title: Exercise 171
statement: An investor pays P for an annuity which provides payments of 100 at the beginning of each month for 10 years. These payments are invested at a nominal annual interest rate of 12% convertible monthly. Monthly interest payments are reinvested at a nominal annual interest rate of 6% convertible monthly. The annual yield rate over the 10-year period is 8% effective. Calculate P.
solution_steps: There are \(10(12)=120\) monthly payments of \(100\).
The payments are invested at a nominal annual interest rate of \(12\%\) convertible monthly, so the monthly interest rate is \(0.12/12=0.01\).
Each payment of \(100\) earns monthly interest of \(100(0.01)=1\).
The monthly interest payments are reinvested at a nominal annual interest rate of \(6\%\) convertible monthly, so the monthly reinvestment rate is \(0.06/12=0.005\).
At the end of 10 years, the original 120 payments contribute \(100(120)=12000\).
The reinvested interest payments form an increasing annuity: \(1,2,3,\ldots,120\).
Thus, the accumulated value of the reinvested interest is \((Is)_{\overline{120}|0.005}\).
Therefore, the total accumulated value is \(100(120)+(Is)_{\overline{120}|0.005}\).
The investor wants an annual effective yield rate of \(8\%\), so the purchase price \(P\) must accumulate to \(P(1.08)^{10}\).
Using \(\text{what I have}=\text{what I demand}\), write \(P(1.08)^{10}=100(120)+(Is)_{\overline{120}|0.005}\).
The closest answer is \(9700\).
generalization: When cash flows are invested in one fund and the interest payments are reinvested in a separate fund, separate the original payments from the reinvested interest. Then use the yield condition by equating the accumulated value of what the investor has to the purchase price accumulated at the desired yield rate.
answer_text: The purchase price is approximately \(\boxed{9699.16}\), so the closest answer is C: \(9700\).

title: Exercise 172
statement: Frances borrows 10000 and agrees to make 20 equal payments where the first payment is due in one year. Each year, she will pay interest at 12% effective on the outstanding principal. The lender wishes to sell the loan to an investor immediately after the loan is made. Determine the sale price such that the investor will achieve a yield of 15% on this investment.
solution_steps: First find Frances's yearly payment.
The loan is repaid with 20 equal annual payments, and the loan interest rate is \(12\%\) effective.
Therefore, \(R=\frac{10000}{a_{\overline{20}|0.12}}\).
Now look at the investor's cash flows.
At time \(0\), the investor pays the sale price \(P\).
At times \(1,2,\ldots,20\), the investor receives annual payments of \(1338.79\).
The investor wants a yield rate of \(15\%\). Therefore, the sale price is the present value of these 20 payments at \(15\%\).
Therefore, the sale price is less than \(8400\).
generalization: When a loan is sold immediately after it is made, first determine the borrower's scheduled payments using the original loan rate. Then price the loan from the investor's perspective by discounting those payments at the investor's desired yield rate.
answer_text: The sale price is \(\boxed{8379.93}\), so the correct answer is A: less than \(8400\).

title: Exercise 173
statement: A loan is repaid with 20 annual payments of 1000, with the first payment one year from now. The interest rate is 6%. Find the loan balance after the 15th payment using: (a) the prospective method; (b) the retrospective method; (c) the BA II Plus AMORT worksheet.
solution_steps: The loan has 20 annual payments of 1000 and an annual effective interest rate of 6%.
We want the outstanding loan balance immediately after the 15th payment, denoted \(OB_{15}\).
Using the prospective method, after the 15th payment there are \(20-15=5\) payments remaining.
Using the retrospective method, first find the original loan amount: \(L=1000a_{\overline{20}|0.06}=11469.92\).
At time 15, the accumulated value of the original loan is \(11469.92(1.06)^{15}\).
The accumulated value of the first 15 payments is \(1000s_{\overline{15}|0.06}\).
Using the BA II Plus, enter \(N=20\), \(I/Y=6\), \(PMT=1000\), and \(FV=0\), then compute \(PV=-11469.92122\).
In the AMORT worksheet, set \(P1=15\) and \(P2=15\).
The calculator gives \(BAL=-4212.363786\), so the outstanding loan balance is \(4212.36\).
generalization: The prospective method finds the outstanding loan balance as the present value of the remaining payments. The retrospective method finds the balance as the accumulated value of the original loan minus the accumulated value of the payments already made. Both methods give the same outstanding balance.
answer_text: The outstanding loan balance after the 15th payment is \(\boxed{4212.36}\).

title: Exercise 174
statement: A loan of 4724.80 is repaid in annual payments of 1000, 1500, and 3000, with the first payment one year from now. The interest rate is 5% for year 1, 10% for year 2, and 5% for year 3. Build the amortization schedule for this loan.
correct: The amortization schedule has balances 4724.80, 3961.04, 2857.14, and 0.00.
solution_steps: The initial outstanding balance is \(OB_0=4724.80\).
For year 1, the interest rate is \(5\%\), so \(I_1=4724.80(0.05)=236.24\).
The first payment is \(1000\), so the principal paid is \(P_1=1000-236.24=763.76\).
The outstanding balance after the first payment is \(OB_1=4724.80-763.76=3961.04\).
For year 2, the interest rate is \(10\%\), so \(I_2=3961.04(0.10)=396.10\).
The second payment is \(1500\), so the principal paid is \(P_2=1500-396.10=1103.90\).
The outstanding balance after the second payment is \(OB_2=3961.04-1103.90=2857.14\).
For year 3, the interest rate is \(5\%\), so \(I_3=2857.14(0.05)=142.86\).
The third payment is \(3000\), so the principal paid is \(P_3=3000-142.86=2857.14\).
The outstanding balance after the third payment is \(OB_3=2857.14-2857.14=0.00\).

Thus, the amortization schedule is: time 0 balance \(4724.80\); time 1 payment \(1000\), interest \(236.24\), principal \(763.76\), balance \(3961.04\); time 2 payment \(1500\), interest \(396.10\), principal \(1103.90\), balance \(2857.14\); time 3 payment \(3000\), interest \(142.86\), principal \(2857.14\), balance \(0.00\).
generalization: When interest rates vary by year, use the applicable rate for each period. The interest paid in year \(t\) is \(I_t=OB_{t-1}i_t\), the principal paid is \(P_t=R_t-I_t\), and the new outstanding balance is \(OB_t=OB_{t-1}-P_t\).
answer_text: The loan is fully amortized after the third payment. The final outstanding balance is \(\boxed{0.00}\).

title: Exercise 175
statement: On December 31, 1984, Smith borrowed 5000 to be repaid in four years with level payments made at the end of every quarter. The first payment was made on March 31, 1985. The effective annual interest rate was 4%. What was the amount of interest paid in 1986?
solution_steps: The loan is repaid over 4 years with quarterly payments, so the number of payments is \(4(4)=16\).
The effective annual interest rate is \(4\%\), so the effective quarterly interest rate is \(j=(1.04)^{1/4}-1\).
This gives \(j=0.0098534\).
Let \(R\) be the quarterly payment. The loan equation is \(5000=Ra_{\overline{16}|j}\).
Therefore, \(R=\frac{5000}{a_{\overline{16}|j}}\).
The first four payments are made in 1985, so payments 1 through 4 correspond to 1985.
The next four payments are made in 1986, so payments 5 through 8 correspond to 1986.
Using the BA II Plus AMORT worksheet, enter \(N=16\), \(I/Y=100((1.04)^{1/4}-1)\), \(PV=-5000\), and \(FV=0\).
Then use \(P1=5\) and \(P2=8\) in the AMORT worksheet.
The calculator gives \(INT=132.7094727\).
Therefore, the amount of interest paid in 1986 is \(132.71\).
generalization: For amortization problems asking for total interest paid over a calendar year, identify which payment numbers fall in that year. Then use the AMORT worksheet with \(P1\) equal to the first relevant payment number and \(P2\) equal to the last relevant payment number.
answer_text: The amount of interest paid in 1986 is \(\boxed{132.71}\).

title: Exercise 176
statement: Todd has a 100000 25-year mortgage with a 12% nominal interest rate convertible monthly. The first payment is due one month after the mortgage is taken out. Twelve years after taking out the mortgage, after making his 144th payment, he refinances with a new nominal interest rate of 8%, again convertible monthly. The new mortgage will be paid off on the same date as the original one. Calculate the difference in the monthly mortgage payment after refinancing.
solution_steps: The original mortgage is for 25 years with monthly payments, so the total number of payments is \(25(12)=300\).
The original nominal annual interest rate is \(12\%\), convertible monthly, so the original monthly interest rate is \(j_1=0.12/12=0.01\).
Let \(X\) be the original monthly mortgage payment. Then \(100000=Xa_{\overline{300}|0.01}\).
After 12 years, Todd has made \(12(12)=144\) payments.
The number of remaining payments is \(300-144=156\).
The outstanding balance immediately after the 144th payment is the present value of the remaining 156 payments at the original monthly rate: \(OB_{144}=1053.22a_{\overline{156}|0.01}\).
The new nominal annual interest rate is \(8\%\), convertible monthly, so the new monthly rate is \(j_2=0.08/12\).
Let \(Y\) be the new monthly payment. Since the new mortgage ends on the same date as the original one, there are still 156 payments remaining.
Solving gives \(Y=857.64\).
Therefore, the difference in monthly payments is \(1053.22-857.64=195.58\).
generalization: For refinancing problems, first find the original payment using the original loan terms. Then find the outstanding balance at the refinancing date. Finally, use the new interest rate and the remaining term to compute the new payment. The payment difference is the original payment minus the new payment.
answer_text: The difference in the monthly mortgage payment is \(\boxed{195.58}\), so the correct answer is C.

title: Exercise 177
statement: A 3000 loan at 18% per year is being repaid by 10 equal annual payments, the first payment due one year after the loan is made. Determine how much the interest part of the sixth payment is.
solution_steps: First find the level annual payment \(R\).
The loan is repaid with 10 equal annual payments at an annual effective interest rate of \(18\%\), so \(3000=Ra_{\overline{10}|0.18}\).
The interest part of the sixth payment is the interest due during year 6.
Therefore, we need the outstanding balance immediately after the fifth payment, \(OB_5\).
Using the prospective method, after the fifth payment there are \(10-5=5\) payments remaining.
Hence, \(OB_5=Ra_{\overline{5}|0.18}\).
The interest part of the sixth payment is \(I_6=OB_5(0.18)\).
Therefore, the interest part of the sixth payment is \(375.75\).
generalization: To find the interest part of a specific loan payment, first find the outstanding balance immediately before that payment. Then multiply that outstanding balance by the interest rate for the period. For payment \(t\), the interest part is \(I_t=OB_{t-1}i\).
answer_text: The interest part of the sixth payment is \(\boxed{375.75}\), so the correct answer is E: at least \(360\).

title: Exercise 178
statement: Kevin takes out a 10-year loan of L, which he repays by the amortization method at an annual effective interest rate of i. Kevin makes payments of 1000 at the end of each year. The total amount of interest repaid during the life of the loan is also equal to L. Calculate the amount of interest repaid during the first year of the loan.
solution_steps: Kevin makes 10 annual payments of \(1000\), so the total amount paid over the life of the loan is \(10(1000)=10000\).
The total interest paid is equal to total payments minus the original loan amount, so \(\text{Total interest}=10000-L\).
We are told that the total amount of interest repaid during the life of the loan is also equal to \(L\).
Solving gives \(2L=10000\), so \(L=5000\).
Now use the loan equation \(5000=1000a_{\overline{10}|i}\).
Therefore, \(a_{\overline{10}|i}=5\).
Thus, \(i\) satisfies \(\frac{1-(1+i)^{-10}}{i}=5\).
Using a financial calculator with \(N=10\), \(PV=5000\), \(PMT=-1000\), and \(FV=0\), we get \(I/Y=15.09841448\).
The interest paid during the first year is \(I_1=Li\).
The closest answer is \(755\).
generalization: For an amortized loan, total interest paid over the life of the loan equals total payments minus the original loan amount. Once the original loan amount and payment amount are known, solve for the interest rate using the annuity equation. The interest paid during the first year is the original loan amount multiplied by the annual effective interest rate.
answer_text: The interest repaid during the first year is approximately \(\boxed{754.92}\), so the closest answer is C: \(755\).

title: Exercise 179
statement: A loan is repaid with 10 annual payments of 1295.05, with the first payment one year after the loan is issued. The loan rate is an annual effective rate of 5%. The interest portion of the n-th payment is 328.67. Determine n.
solution_steps: Let \(N\) be the total number of payments and let \(t\) be the payment number we want to find.
The discount factor is \(v=\frac{1}{1.05}\).
We are given that the interest portion of the \(t\)-th payment is \(I_t=328.67\).
Since \(R=I_t+P_t\), the principal portion of the \(t\)-th payment is \(P_t=1295.05-328.67=966.38\).
For a level-payment loan, the principal portion of the \(t\)-th payment is \(P_t=Rv^{N-t+1}\).
Solving gives \(t=5\).
generalization: For a level-payment loan, the principal portion of the \(t\)-th payment is \(P_t=Rv^{N-t+1}\). If the interest portion is given, first compute the principal portion using \(P_t=R-I_t\), then solve for the payment number.
answer_text: The payment number is \(\boxed{5}\).

title: Exercise 180
statement: Joe negotiates an 8-year loan which requires him to pay 1200 per month for the first 4 years and 1500 per month for the remaining years. The interest rate is 13%, convertible monthly, and the first payment is due in one month. Determine the amount of principal in the 17th payment.
solution_steps: The nominal annual interest rate is \(13\%\), convertible monthly, so the monthly interest rate is \(j=0.13/12\).
The loan lasts 8 years, so there are \(8(12)=96\) monthly payments.
The first 4 years correspond to \(4(12)=48\) payments.
Thus, payments 1 through 48 are \(1200\), and payments 49 through 96 are \(1500\).
We want the amount of principal in the 17th payment.
Since the 17th payment is one of the first 48 payments, \(R_{17}=1200\).
The principal portion is \(P_{17}=R_{17}-I_{17}\), so we need the interest portion of the 17th payment.
The interest portion of the 17th payment is based on the outstanding balance immediately after the 16th payment, denoted \(OB_{16}\).
After the 16th payment, there are \(96-16=80\) payments remaining.
Imagine all 80 remaining payments are \(1500\), then subtract \(300\) from the first \(48-16=32\) payments.
Therefore, \(OB_{16}=1500a_{\overline{80}|j}-300a_{\overline{32}|j}\).
The interest portion of the 17th payment is \(I_{17}=OB_{16}j=71911.10(0.13/12)=779.04\).
generalization: For non-level loan payments, use first principles. To find the principal portion of a payment, first find the outstanding balance immediately before that payment. Then compute the interest portion using \(I_t=OB_{t-1}i\), and subtract from the payment: \(P_t=R_t-I_t\).
answer_text: The amount of principal in the 17th payment is \(\boxed{420.96}\).

title: Exercise 181
statement: Larry is repaying a loan with payments of 2500 at the end of every two years. If the amount of interest in the fourth installment is 2458, find the amount of principal in the seventh installment. Assume an annual effective interest rate of 13%.
solution_steps: Payments are made every two years, so first compute the effective interest rate per two-year payment period.
The effective two-year rate is \(j=(1.13)^2-1=0.2769\).
Each installment is \(2500\).
The interest portion of the fourth installment is \(I_4=2458\).
Therefore, the principal portion of the fourth installment is \(P_4=2500-2458=42\).
For a level-payment loan, principal payments grow at the effective interest rate per payment period.
The seventh installment is three payment periods after the fourth installment, so \(P_7=P_4(1+j)^3\).
Therefore, \(P_7=87.44\).
generalization: For a level-payment loan, the principal portions of successive payments grow geometrically at the effective interest rate per payment period. If payments are made every two years, use the effective two-year interest rate.
answer_text: The amount of principal in the seventh installment is \(\boxed{87.44}\).

title: Exercise 182
statement: A loan is repaid in ten equal annual installments with the first installment paid one year after the loan is made. The effective annual interest rate is 4%. The total amount of principal repaid in the fifth, sixth, and seventh payments combined is 6083. What is the total amount of interest paid in the second, third, and fourth payments combined?
solution_steps: For a level-payment loan, the principal portions grow at the loan interest rate.
Since the effective annual interest rate is \(4\%\), we have \(P_{t+1}=P_t(1.04)\).
We are given \(P_5+P_6+P_7=6083\).
Solving gives \(P_1=1665.74\).
The original loan amount is the sum of all principal repayments: \(L=P_1+P_2+\cdots+P_{10}\).
Since the principal payments grow geometrically, \(L=P_1s_{\overline{10}|0.04}\).
Now find the level annual payment \(R\) using \(20000=Ra_{\overline{10}|0.04}\).
Solving gives \(R=2465.82\).
We want \(I_2+I_3+I_4\).
generalization: For a level-payment amortized loan, principal payments grow geometrically at the loan interest rate. Once one principal payment is known, all other principal portions can be found by compounding forward or discounting backward. The interest portion of each payment is then \(I_t=R-P_t\).
answer_text: The total amount of interest paid in the second, third, and fourth payments is \(\boxed{1989.44}\), so the correct answer is A: less than \(2000\).

title: Exercise 183
statement: Sam borrowed 1000 on January 1, 1993, to be repaid by level payments every two years beginning January 1, 1995, at an effective annual rate of interest of 9%. The amount of interest in the fourth installment is 177.72. Determine the amount of principal in the sixth installment.
solution_steps: Payments are made every two years, so first compute the effective two-year interest rate.
The annual effective interest rate is \(9\%\), so \(j=(1.09)^2-1=0.1881\).
Let \(R\) be the level installment payment.
The fourth installment is paid at time 8, so its interest portion is based on the outstanding balance immediately after the third installment.
Using the retrospective method, \(OB_3=1000(1+j)^3-Rs_{\overline{3}|j}\).
The interest portion of the fourth installment is \(I_4=OB_3j\).
Solving gives \(R=203.43\).
The principal portion of the fourth installment is \(P_4=R-I_4=203.43-177.72=25.71\).
For a level-payment loan, principal portions grow at the effective interest rate per payment period.
The sixth installment is two payment periods after the fourth installment, so \(P_6=P_4(1+j)^2\).
generalization: When payments occur every two years, convert the annual effective interest rate into the effective rate per two-year payment period. For a level-payment amortized loan, principal portions grow geometrically at the loan interest rate per payment period.
answer_text: The amount of principal in the sixth installment is \(\boxed{36.29}\), so the correct answer is C.

title: Exercise 184
statement: Donald takes out a loan to be repaid with annual payments of 500 at the end of each year for 2n years. The annual effective interest rate is 4.94%. The sum of the interest paid in year 1 plus the interest paid in year n + 1 is equal to 720. Calculate the amount of interest paid in year 10.
solution_steps: The loan has level annual payments of \(R=500\) for \(2n\) years.
For a level-payment amortized loan, the principal portion of the \(t\)-th payment is \(P_t=Rv^{2n-t+1}\).
Since \(I_t=R-P_t\), the interest portion of the \(t\)-th payment is \(I_t=500-500v^{2n-t+1}\).
We are given \(I_1+I_{n+1}=720\).
Rearranging gives \(v^{2n}+v^n=0.56\).
Solving the quadratic gives the positive solution \(x=0.4\).
Therefore, \(v^n=0.4\).
The closest answer is \(376\).
generalization: For a level-payment amortized loan, the principal portion of the \(t\)-th payment is \(P_t=Rv^{N-t+1}\), where \(N\) is the total number of payments. The interest portion is then \(I_t=R-P_t\). This allows relationships involving interest payments to be converted into equations involving powers of \(v\).
answer_text: The amount of interest paid in year 10 is approximately \(\boxed{376.53}\), so the correct answer is D: \(376\).

title: Exercise 185
statement: A 1000 loan at 3.5% annual effective rate of interest can be repaid by either: (i) 11 annual payments of 100 plus a final balloon payment, or (ii) 12 annual payments of 100 plus a final drop payment. Determine the difference between the balloon payment and the drop payment.
solution_steps: Suppose that \(100\) is paid for 12 years.
The outstanding balance immediately after the 12th payment is \(OB_{12}=1000(1.035)^{12}-100s_{\overline{12}|0.035}\).
For the balloon payment option, the borrower makes 11 payments of \(100\), followed by a final balloon payment at time 12.
If a regular payment of \(100\) were made at time 12, the remaining balance after that payment would be \(50.87\).
Therefore, the balloon payment is \(100+50.87=150.87\).
For the drop payment option, the borrower makes 12 payments of \(100\), followed by a smaller final drop payment.
After the 12th payment, the outstanding balance is \(50.87\).
This balance must be accumulated one more year to the final drop payment date.
Therefore, the drop payment is \(50.87(1.035)=52.65\).
The difference between the balloon payment and the drop payment is \(150.87-52.65=98.22\).
generalization: A balloon payment is larger than the regular payment because it includes the regular payment plus the remaining outstanding balance. A drop payment is smaller than the regular payment and is usually found by accumulating the outstanding balance after the last regular payment to the final payment date.
answer_text: The difference between the balloon payment and the drop payment is \(\boxed{98.22}\).

title: Exercise 186
statement: For the previous exercise, calculate the absolute difference between the total interest paid in the two repayment options.
solution_steps: From the previous exercise, the balloon payment is \(150.87\) and the drop payment is \(52.65\).
The total interest paid is equal to total loan payments minus the loan amount.
Under the balloon payment option, the borrower makes 11 payments of \(100\), plus a balloon payment of \(150.87\).
Thus, the total interest paid under the balloon option is \(100(11)+150.87-1000=250.87\).
Under the drop payment option, the borrower makes 12 payments of \(100\), plus a drop payment of \(52.65\).
Thus, the total interest paid under the drop option is \(100(12)+52.65-1000=252.65\).
The absolute difference is \(|252.65-250.87|=1.78\).
generalization: For any loan, total interest paid equals total payments minus the original loan amount. To compare two repayment options, compute the total payments under each option, subtract the same original loan amount, and then take the absolute difference.
answer_text: The absolute difference between the total interest paid in the two repayment options is \(\boxed{1.78}\).

title: Exercise 187
statement: A loan of 10000 will be paid back in N months. Payments of 250 will be made at the end of each month for the first N - 1 months. At the end of the N-th month, a smaller payment of P will be made. Assuming an interest rate of 12% per annum convertible monthly, what is the amount of the last payment P?
solution_steps: The nominal annual interest rate is \(12\%\), convertible monthly, so the monthly interest rate is \(i=0.12/12=0.01\).
First determine how many full payments of \(250\) are needed.
Thus, \(a_{\overline{n}|0.01}=40\).
Therefore, \(v^n=0.60\).
Solving gives \(n=51.33\).
This means that 51 full payments of \(250\) are made, followed by a smaller final payment at month 52.
The equation of value at time \(0\) is \(250a_{\overline{51}|0.01}+Pv^{52}=10000\).
Solving for \(P\), we get \(P=\frac{10000-250a_{\overline{51}|0.01}}{v^{52}}\).
Therefore, \(80<P\leq 90\).
generalization: For a loan repaid by regular payments plus a smaller final drop payment, first find the non-integer number of full regular payments. The integer part gives the number of full payments, and the final smaller payment is found by writing an equation of value that includes the full payments and the final drop payment.
answer_text: The last payment is \(\boxed{84.67}\), so the correct answer is B: \(80<P\leq 90\).

title: Exercise 188
statement: Sally takes out a 7000 loan. The original terms of the loan require 60 equal monthly payments starting at the end of the first month at a nominal annual interest rate of 8% convertible monthly. The lender offers an alternative payment scheme: Sally would make no payments for the first 5 months, followed by the same monthly payment as the original terms of the loan. She will still repay the entire loan in 60 months by making an extra payment at the end of the last month. Determine the amount of the extra payment to be made at the end of the 60th month.
solution_steps: The nominal annual interest rate is \(8\%\), convertible monthly, so the monthly interest rate is \(i=0.08/12\).
First find the original monthly payment. Let this payment be \(X\).
The original loan is repaid with 60 equal monthly payments, so \(7000=Xa_{\overline{60}|0.08/12}\).
Under the alternative payment scheme, Sally makes no payments for the first 5 months.
Then she makes payments of \(141.935\) from month 6 through month 60, giving \(55\) regular monthly payments.
Let \(Y\) be the extra payment made at the end of month 60.
Write an equation of value at time 60.
The accumulated value of the original loan at time 60 is \(7000(1+0.08/12)^{60}\).
The accumulated value of the 55 regular payments from months 6 through 60 is \(141.935s_{\overline{55}|0.08/12}\).
generalization: When a payment schedule is delayed but the loan must still be repaid by the original maturity date, write an equation of value at the final repayment date. Accumulate the original loan to that date and equate it to the accumulated value of the actual payments plus any final extra payment.
answer_text: The extra payment is \(\boxed{1036.47}\), so the correct answer is E: at least \(1000\).

title: Exercise 189
statement: A 10000 loan is being paid off by annual payments of 2000 plus a smaller final payment. If the annual effective rate of interest is 15%, and the first payment is made one year after the time of the loan, find the amount of interest, X, contained in the fifth payment.
solution_steps: The annual effective interest rate is \(i=0.15\).
The interest contained in the fifth payment is based on the outstanding balance immediately after the fourth payment.
Using the retrospective method, \(OB_4=10000(1.15)^4-2000s_{\overline{4}|0.15}\).
The interest contained in the fifth payment is \(I_5=OB_4(0.15)\).
Therefore, \(X=1125.50\).
generalization: To find the interest portion of a payment, first find the outstanding balance immediately before that payment. For the fifth payment, find the balance immediately after the fourth payment, then multiply by the interest rate.
answer_text: The amount of interest contained in the fifth payment is \(\boxed{1125.50}\), so the correct answer is E: \(1100<X\).

title: Exercise 190
statement: Name two ways corporations raise capital.
solution_steps: Corporations usually raise capital in two main ways.
The first way is to issue debt, such as bonds.
The second way is to issue equity, such as common stock.
Debt means the corporation borrows money from investors and usually promises to make interest payments and repay principal.
Equity means investors buy an ownership interest in the company, but the return is not guaranteed.
generalization: Corporations raise capital mainly by issuing debt or issuing equity. Debt creates a borrowing obligation, while equity represents ownership.
answer_text: The two ways corporations raise capital are \(\boxed{\text{issue debt and issue equity}}\).

title: Exercise 191
statement: Company XYZ issues common stock, preferred stock, and bonds. Rank the degree of security from most secure to least secure for the three financial instruments.
solution_steps: The degree of security depends on the priority of claims.
Bondholders are generally paid before stockholders.
Preferred stockholders are paid after bondholders, but before common stockholders.
Common stockholders have the residual claim, meaning they are paid only after the obligations to bondholders and preferred stockholders have been met.
Therefore, the ranking from most secure to least secure is bonds, preferred stock, and common stock.
generalization: In terms of claim priority, debt is generally more secure than equity. Preferred stock has priority over common stock, but bonds have priority over both.
answer_text: The ranking from most secure to least secure is \(\boxed{\text{Bonds, Preferred Stock, Common Stock}}\).

title: Exercise 192
statement: Which of the following correctly orders the length of maturity for the debt securities below, ordered from shortest maturity to longest maturity?
solution_steps: Debt securities are often classified by their time to maturity.
A bill has the shortest maturity, usually one year or less.
A note has an intermediate maturity, usually greater than one year and up to ten years.
A bond has the longest maturity, usually greater than ten years.
Therefore, from shortest maturity to longest maturity, the correct order is Bill, Note, Bond.
generalization: For debt securities, bills are short-term, notes are intermediate-term, and bonds are long-term.
answer_text: The correct order is \(\boxed{\text{Bill, Note, Bond}}\), so the correct answer is B.

title: Exercise 193
statement: A 1000 par value twenty-year bond has coupons at 5% convertible semiannually. If the yield rate is 6% convertible semiannually, what is the bond's price?
solution_steps: The par value is \(F=1000\).
The annual coupon rate is \(5\%\), convertible semiannually, so the coupon rate per semiannual period is \(r=0.05/2=0.025\).
The semiannual coupon payment is \(Fr=1000(0.025)=25\).
The bond lasts 20 years and coupons are paid semiannually, so the number of coupon payments is \(n=20(2)=40\).
The yield rate is \(6\%\), convertible semiannually, so the yield rate per coupon period is \(i=0.06/2=0.03\).
Unless stated otherwise, the redemption value is assumed to equal the par value, so \(C=1000\).
Using the basic bond price formula, \(P=Fra_{\overline{n}|i}+Cv_i^n\).
Substitute the values: \(P=25a_{\overline{40}|0.03}+1000v_{0.03}^{40}\).
This gives \(P=884.43\).
generalization: The price of a bond is the present value of its coupon payments plus the present value of its redemption value. When coupons and yield are convertible at the same frequency, convert both rates to the rate per coupon period.
answer_text: The bond's price is \(\boxed{884.43}\).

title: Exercise 194
statement: A 9% bond with a 1000 par value and coupons payable semiannually is redeemable at maturity for 1100. At a purchase price of P, the bond yields a nominal annual interest rate of 8%, compounded semiannually, and the present value of the redemption amount is 190. Determine P.
solution_steps: The par value is \(F=1000\).
The annual coupon rate is \(9\%\), payable semiannually, so the coupon rate per semiannual period is \(r=0.09/2=0.045\).
The semiannual coupon payment is \(Fr=1000(0.045)=45\).
The redemption value is \(C=1100\).
The yield rate is \(8\%\), compounded semiannually, so the yield rate per coupon period is \(i=0.08/2=0.04\).
The present value of the redemption amount is \(K=190\).
Solving gives \(n=44.77\).
Since the number of coupon payments must be an integer, and the given value \(190\) is likely rounded, use \(n=45\).
Using the basic bond price formula, \(P=Fra_{\overline{n}|i}+Cv_i^n\).
Substitute the values: \(P=45a_{\overline{45}|0.04}+1100v_{0.04}^{45}\).
This gives \(P=1120.72\).
Alternatively, using Makeham's formula, \(P=K+\frac{g}{i}(C-K)\), where \(g=Fr/C=45/1100\).
The small difference is due to rounding in the given value \(K=190\).
generalization: The price of a bond is the present value of its coupon payments plus the present value of its redemption value. If the present value of the redemption value is given, it can be used to infer the number of coupon periods or applied directly in Makeham's formula.
answer_text: The bond price is approximately \(\boxed{1120.72}\).

title: Exercise 195
statement: An insurance company owns a 1000 par value 10% bond with semiannual coupons. The bond will mature for 1000 at the end of 10 years. The company decides that an 8-year bond would be preferable. Current yield rates are 7% compounded semiannually. The company uses the proceeds from the sale of the 10% bond to purchase a 6% bond with semiannual coupons, maturing at par at the end of 8 years. Calculate the par value of the 8-year bond.
solution_steps: First find the selling price of the 10-year bond.
For the 10-year bond, \(F=1000\) and \(C=1000\).
The annual coupon rate is \(10\%\), with semiannual coupons, so the coupon rate per half-year is \(r=0.10/2=0.05\).
Thus, each coupon payment is \(Fr=1000(0.05)=50\).
The bond has 10 years remaining, with semiannual coupons, so \(n=10(2)=20\).
The current yield rate is \(7\%\) compounded semiannually, so the yield rate per half-year is \(i=0.07/2=0.035\).
Using the basic bond price formula, \(P=Fra_{\overline{n}|i}+Cv_i^n\).
The insurance company uses these proceeds to buy an 8-year bond.
Let \(F\) be the par value of the new 8-year bond.
Since the new bond matures at par, \(C=F\).
The annual coupon rate is \(6\%\), with semiannual coupons, so \(r=0.06/2=0.03\).
Therefore, each coupon payment is \(Fr=0.03F\).
The new bond lasts 8 years, with semiannual coupons, so \(n=8(2)=16\).
The yield rate is still \(i=0.035\).
The price of the new bond must equal the proceeds from the sale of the old bond.
Solving gives \(F=1291.27\).
The closest answer is \(1290\).
generalization: When proceeds from one bond are used to purchase another bond, first price the bond being sold using the current yield rate. Then set that amount equal to the price of the new bond and solve for the unknown quantity, such as the par value.
answer_text: The par value of the 8-year bond is approximately \(\boxed{1291.27}\), so the closest answer is B: \(1290\).

title: Exercise 196
statement: A 700 par value five-year 10% bond with semiannual coupons is purchased for 670.60. The present value of the redemption value is 372.05. Calculate the redemption value.
solution_steps: The par value is \(F=700\).
The annual coupon rate is \(10\%\), with semiannual coupons, so the coupon rate per semiannual period is \(r=0.10/2=0.05\).
The semiannual coupon payment is \(Fr=700(0.05)=35\).
The bond lasts 5 years with semiannual coupons, so the number of coupon payments is \(n=5(2)=10\).
The bond price is \(P=670.60\).
The present value of the redemption value is \(K=372.05\).
Using \(P=Fra_{\overline{n}|i}+K\), we get \(670.60=35a_{\overline{10}|i}+372.05\).
Solving \(a_{\overline{10}|i}=8.53\) gives \(i=0.03\).
Now use \(K=Cv_i^n\).
generalization: If the present value of the redemption value is given, use \(K=Cv_i^n\). Sometimes the yield rate \(i\) must first be found from the bond price equation \(P=Fra_{\overline{n}|i}+K\).
answer_text: The redemption value is \(\boxed{500}\), so the correct answer is A.

title: Exercise 197
statement: A 1000 par value 10-year bond with semiannual coupons and redeemable at 1100 is purchased at 1135 to yield 12% convertible semiannually. The first coupon is X. Each subsequent coupon is 4% greater than the preceding coupon. Determine X.
solution_steps: The bond is a 10-year bond with semiannual coupons, so the number of coupon payments is \(n=10(2)=20\).
The bond yields \(12\%\) convertible semiannually, so the yield rate per coupon period is \(i=0.12/2=0.06\).
The redemption value is \(C=1100\).
The first coupon is \(X\), and each subsequent coupon is \(4\%\) greater than the preceding coupon.
Therefore, the coupons are \(X, X(1.04), X(1.04)^2, \ldots, X(1.04)^{19}\).
The price of the bond is the present value of the increasing coupons plus the present value of the redemption value.
The coupon present value is a geometric annuity.
Solving gives \(X=50\).
generalization: When bond coupons increase by a constant percentage each period, the coupon stream is a geometric annuity. The bond price is the present value of the increasing coupon stream plus the present value of the redemption value.
answer_text: The first coupon is \(\boxed{50}\), so the correct answer is E.

title: Exercise 198
statement: True or false? (1) If r > i, then the bond must be a premium bond. (2) If g < i, then the bond must be a discount bond. (3) If Ki > Fr, then the bond must be a discount bond. (4) If i = r and the bond is redeemable at par, then the bond's price is equal to the par amount.
solution_steps: Statement 1 is false. We cannot determine whether a bond is bought at a premium or at a discount by only comparing \(r\) and \(i\).
The correct comparison is between the actual coupon payment \(Fr\) and the required coupon payment \(Ci\), or equivalently between \(g\) and \(i\).
A bond is bought at a premium if \(Fr>Ci\), or equivalently if \(g>i\).
Therefore, \(r>i\) alone is not enough unless the bond is redeemable at par, so that \(C=F\).
Statement 2 is true. If \(g<i\), then \(Cg<Ci\).
Since \(Fr=Cg\), we get \(Fr<Ci\).
Therefore, the coupon payment is smaller than the required payment, so the bond is bought at a discount.
Statement 3 is true. Recall that \(K=Cv_i^n\), so \(K\) is the present value of the redemption amount.
Since \(v_i^n<1\), we have \(K<C\). Multiplying by \(i\), we get \(Ki<Ci\).
If \(Ki>Fr\), then since \(Ci>Ki\), it follows that \(Ci>Fr\).
Therefore, the coupon payment is smaller than the required payment, so the bond is a discount bond.
Statement 4 is true. If the bond is redeemable at par, then \(C=F\).
If also \(i=r\), then \(Ci=Fi=Fr\).
Therefore, \(Fr-Ci=0\).
Using the premium / discount formula \(P=C+(Fr-Ci)a_{\overline{n}|i}\), we get \(P=C\).
Since \(C=F\), the bond's price is equal to the par amount.
generalization: To decide whether a bond is bought at a premium or discount, compare the actual coupon payment \(Fr\) with the required coupon payment \(Ci\), or compare the modified coupon rate \(g=Fr/C\) with the yield rate \(i\).
answer_text: The answers are \(\boxed{\text{False, True, True, True}}\).

title: Exercise 199
statement: A fifteen-year 1000 bond with quarterly coupons of 25 is redeemable for 1200. The bond is priced to yield 8% convertible quarterly. Determine the amount of the premium or discount.
solution_steps: The bond has quarterly coupons for 15 years, so the number of coupon payments is \(n=15(4)=60\).
The yield rate is \(8\%\) convertible quarterly, so the yield rate per quarter is \(i=0.08/4=0.02\).
The redemption value is \(C=1200\).
The quarterly coupon payment is given directly as \(Fr=25\).
The required coupon payment for the bond to be priced at par is \(Ci=1200(0.02)=24\).
Since \(Fr>Ci\), the bond is bought at a premium.
The premium is \(P-C=(Fr-Ci)a_{\overline{n}|i}\).
Thus, \(\text{Premium}=(25-24)a_{\overline{60}|0.02}\).
Therefore, \(\text{Premium}=34.76\).
Alternatively, calculate the price directly: \(P=25a_{\overline{60}|0.02}+1200v_{0.02}^{60}=1234.76\).
generalization: To determine whether a bond is bought at a premium or discount, compare the coupon payment \(Fr\) with the required payment \(Ci\). If \(Fr>Ci\), the bond is bought at a premium. If \(Fr<Ci\), the bond is bought at a discount.
answer_text: The bond is bought at a premium of \(\boxed{34.76}\).

title: Exercise 200
statement: For a ten-year bond with semiannual coupons, you are given: C = 1200, r = 0.04, g = 0.03, and i = 0.04. Determine the bond premium or discount.
solution_steps: The bond has semiannual coupons for 10 years, so the number of coupon payments is \(n=10(2)=20\).
Since \(g<i\), the bond is bought at a discount.
Using \(Fr=Cg\), the discount formula is \(\text{Discount}=(Ci-Fr)a_{\overline{n}|i}\).
Since \(Fr=Cg\), this becomes \(\text{Discount}=(Ci-Cg)a_{\overline{n}|i}\).
Factor out \(C\): \(\text{Discount}=C(i-g)a_{\overline{n}|i}\).
Substitute the values: \(\text{Discount}=1200(0.04-0.03)a_{\overline{20}|0.04}\).
Thus, \(\text{Discount}=12a_{\overline{20}|0.04}\).
generalization: When using the modified coupon rate \(g\), compare \(g\) with the yield rate \(i\). If \(g>i\), the bond is bought at a premium. If \(g<i\), the bond is bought at a discount. The discount can be computed as \(C(i-g)a_{\overline{n}|i}\).
answer_text: The bond is bought at a discount of \(\boxed{163.08}\).

title: Exercise 201
statement: You are given: (i) A 10-year 8% semiannual coupon bond is purchased at a discount of X. (ii) A 10-year 9% semiannual coupon bond is purchased at a premium of Y. (iii) A 10-year 10% semiannual coupon bond is purchased at a premium of 2X. (iv) All bonds were purchased at the same yield rate and have par values of 1000. Calculate Y.
solution_steps: All bonds have par value \(F=1000\). Since the bonds are redeemed at par, \(C=1000\).
All bonds are 10-year bonds with semiannual coupons, so \(n=10(2)=20\).
Let \(i\) be the yield rate per semiannual period.
The premium / discount formula is \(P=C+(Fr-Ci)a_{\overline{n}|i}\).
Therefore, for a premium, \(P-C=(Fr-Ci)a_{\overline{n}|i}\), and for a discount, \(C-P=(Ci-Fr)a_{\overline{n}|i}\).
For the 8% bond, the semiannual coupon is \(1000(0.08/2)=40\). Since it is purchased at a discount of \(X\), we have \(X=(1000i-40)a_{\overline{20}|i}\).
For the 9% bond, the semiannual coupon is \(1000(0.09/2)=45\). Since it is purchased at a premium of \(Y\), we have \(Y=(45-1000i)a_{\overline{20}|i}\).
For the 10% bond, the semiannual coupon is \(1000(0.10/2)=50\). Since it is purchased at a premium of \(2X\), we have \(2X=(50-1000i)a_{\overline{20}|i}\).
Substitute \(X=(1000i-40)a_{\overline{20}|i}\) into the equation for the 10% bond: \(2(1000i-40)a_{\overline{20}|i}=(50-1000i)a_{\overline{20}|i}\).
Therefore, \(Y=(1/2)X\).
generalization: When several bonds have the same maturity, redemption value, and yield rate, their premiums and discounts are proportional to the difference between their coupon payment \(Fr\) and the required payment \(Ci\). This allows ratios of premiums and discounts to be compared without calculating the annuity factor.
answer_text: The value of \(Y\) is \(\boxed{X/2}\), so the correct answer is C.

title: Exercise 202
statement: A 1000 par value bond redeemable in 20 years at par with 6% annual coupons is purchased to produce an annual yield of 5%. The purchaser values the bond for accounting purposes at its amortized value. Calculate the amortized value of the bond at the end of the 5th year.
solution_steps: The bond has par value \(F=1000\).
Since the bond is redeemable at par, \(C=1000\).
The annual coupon rate is \(6\%\), so the annual coupon payment is \(Fr=1000(0.06)=60\).
The annual yield rate is \(i=0.05\).
The amortized value at the end of the 5th year is the book value at time 5.
At time 5, there are \(20-5=15\) future coupon payments remaining.
The amortized value is the present value at time 5 of the future bond payments.
Alternatively, first calculate the purchase price: \(P=60a_{\overline{20}|0.05}+1000v_{0.05}^{20}=1124.622103\).
Using the AMORT worksheet with \(P1=5\), \(P2=5\), gives \(BAL=-1103.79658\).
The negative sign is due to the calculator's cash-flow convention, so \(BV_5=1103.80\).
generalization: The amortized value, or book value, of a bond at time \(t\) is the present value at time \(t\) of the remaining coupons and redemption value. Prospectively, \(BV_t=Fra_{\overline{n-t}|i}+Cv_i^{n-t}\).
answer_text: The amortized value of the bond at the end of the 5th year is \(\boxed{1103.80}\).

title: Exercise 203
statement: A ten-year bond bears semiannual coupons of 4 each and has a redemption value of 100. The bond is purchased to yield 10% compounded semiannually. Find the amount of increase in the book value at the time of the tenth coupon payment.
solution_steps: The bond has semiannual coupons for 10 years, so the number of coupon periods is \(n=10(2)=20\).
The yield rate is \(10\%\) compounded semiannually, so the yield rate per coupon period is \(i=0.10/2=0.05\).
The coupon payment is \(Fr=4\).
The redemption value is \(C=100\).
Since \(Ci=100(0.05)=5\) and \(Fr=4\), we have \(Fr<Ci\).
Therefore, this is a discount bond, and its book value increases over time.
The book value after the 9th coupon payment is the present value at time 9 of the remaining payments.
There are \(20-9=11\) coupon payments remaining, so \(BV_9=4a_{\overline{11}|0.05}+100v_{0.05}^{11}\).
The book value after the 10th coupon payment is \(BV_{10}=4a_{\overline{10}|0.05}+100v_{0.05}^{10}\).
Therefore, the increase in book value is \(BV_{10}-BV_9=92.278-91.694=0.584\).
Alternatively, the interest earned during the 10th period is \(I_{10}=BV_9i=91.694(0.05)=4.5847\).
Since the coupon payment is only \(4\), the book value increases by \(4.5847-4=0.5847\).
generalization: For a discount bond, the coupon payment is smaller than the interest earned on the book value. The difference is a write-up, meaning the book value increases toward the redemption value.
answer_text: The increase in book value at the time of the tenth coupon payment is \(\boxed{0.584}\).

title: Exercise 204
statement: A bond with a par value of 1000 and 6% semiannual coupons is redeemable for 1100. You are given: (i) The bond is purchased at P to yield 8% convertible semiannually. (ii) The amount of principal adjustment for the 16th semiannual period is 5. Calculate P.
solution_steps: The par value is \(F=1000\).
The bond has \(6\%\) semiannual coupons, so the coupon rate per half-year is \(r=0.06/2=0.03\).
Thus, the coupon payment is \(Fr=1000(0.03)=30\).
The redemption value is \(C=1100\).
The bond is purchased to yield \(8\%\) convertible semiannually, so the yield rate per half-year is \(i=0.08/2=0.04\).
The required payment is \(Ci=1100(0.04)=44\).
Since \(Fr<Ci\), this is a discount bond.
For a discount bond, the book value increases over time, so the principal adjustment is negative if we define \(P_t=Fr-I_t\).
The amount of principal adjustment for the 16th semiannual period is \(5\), so \(P_{16}=-5\).
Principal adjustments grow geometrically: \(P_{t+k}=P_t(1+i)^k\).
During the first semiannual period, the interest earned is \(I_1=P(0.04)\).
The first principal adjustment is \(P_1=Fr-I_1=30-0.04P\).
generalization: For bond amortization, the principal adjustments grow geometrically by a factor of \(1+i\). For a discount bond, \(Fr<Ci\), so the adjustment \(P_t=Fr-I_t\) is negative and represents a write-up in book value.
answer_text: The purchase price is \(\boxed{819.41}\).

title: Exercise 205
statement: A 30-year 10000 bond that pays 3% annual coupons matures at par. It is purchased to yield 5% for the first 15 years and 4% thereafter. Calculate the amount for accumulation of discount for year 8.
solution_steps: The bond has face amount \(F=10000\).
It pays \(3\%\) annual coupons, so the annual coupon payment is \(Fr=10000(0.03)=300\).
The bond matures at par, so \(C=10000\).
We want the accumulation of discount for year 8.
Since year 8 is during the first 15 years, the applicable yield rate for that year is \(i=0.05\).
To find the accumulation of discount for year 8, first find the book value at time 7.
At time 15, there are 15 annual coupons remaining, and the bond is valued using the \(4\%\) yield rate.
At time 7, there are 8 years until time 15.
This gives \(BV_7=7954.82\).
The interest due during year 8 is \(I_8=BV_7(0.05)=7954.82(0.05)=397.74\).
The coupon payment is \(Fr=300\).
Thus, the principal adjustment is \(P_8=Fr-I_8=300-397.74=-97.74\).
The negative sign means this is an accumulation of discount, or a write-up.
Therefore, the amount of accumulation of discount is \(97.74\), which rounds to \(98\).
generalization: For a discount bond, the interest earned on the book value is greater than the coupon payment. The difference is the accumulation of discount, also called a write-up. When yield rates change over time, compute the book value by discounting future cash flows using the appropriate yield rates for each future period.
answer_text: The amount of accumulation of discount for year 8 is approximately \(\boxed{97.74}\), so the correct answer is E: \(98\).

title: Exercise 206
statement: Becky buys an n-year 1000 par value bond with 6.5% annual coupons at a price of 825.44. The price assumes an annual effective yield rate of i. The total write-up in book value of the bond during the first 2 years after purchase is 23.76. Calculate i, where i > 0.
solution_steps: The par value is \(F=1000\).
The annual coupon rate is \(6.5\%\), so the annual coupon payment is \(Fr=1000(0.065)=65\).
The purchase price is \(P=825.44\).
The total write-up in book value during the first 2 years is \(23.76\).
Therefore, the book value at time 2 is \(BV_2=825.44+23.76=849.20\).
Now write a value equation at time 0 using the first two coupons and the book value at time 2.
Using a financial calculator with \(N=2\), \(PV=-825.44\), \(PMT=65\), and \(FV=849.20\), we get \(I/Y=9.2502\).
Therefore, \(i=9.2502\%\).
The closest answer is \(9.25\%\).
generalization: When the write-up over a period is given, add it to the initial book value to obtain the later book value. Then use a value equation over that period, treating the later book value as the future value.
answer_text: The annual effective yield rate is approximately \(\boxed{9.25\%}\), so the correct answer is D.

title: Exercise 207
statement: An investor bought a 15-year bond with par value of 100000 and 8% semiannual coupons. The bond is callable at par on any coupon date beginning with the 24th coupon. Find the highest price paid that will yield a rate not less than i^(2) = 6%.
solution_steps: The par value is \(F=100000\).
The bond has \(8\%\) semiannual coupons, so the coupon rate per half-year is \(r=0.08/2=0.04\).
Thus, each coupon payment is \(Fr=100000(0.04)=4000\).
The desired yield rate is \(i^{(2)}=6\%\).
Therefore, the yield rate per half-year is \(i=0.06/2=0.03\).
The bond is callable at par, so the redemption value at any call date is \(C=100000\).
The bond can be called on any coupon date beginning with the 24th coupon. Since the bond is a 15-year bond with semiannual coupons, the final maturity date is the 30th coupon.
Thus, the possible redemption dates are \(n=24,25,\ldots,30\).
Using the premium / discount formula, \(P=C+(Fr-Ci)a_{\overline{n}|i}\).
Therefore, \(P=100000+1000a_{\overline{n}|0.03}\), where \(n=24,25,\ldots,30\).
Since \(Fr-Ci>0\), the bond is a premium bond.
For a premium callable bond, the worst case for the investor is the earliest call date.
Therefore, the minimum price occurs at \(n=24\).
generalization: For a callable bond, calculate the price for each possible redemption date using the desired minimum yield, then choose the minimum price. This guarantees that the investor earns at least the required yield no matter when the bond is called.
answer_text: The highest price paid that will yield a rate not less than \(i^{(2)}=6\%\) is \(\boxed{116935.54}\).

title: Exercise 208
statement: A 1000 par value bond with coupons of 9% payable semiannually was called for 1100 prior to maturity. The bond was bought for 917.44 immediately after a coupon payment and was held to call. The nominal yield rate convertible semiannually was 10%. Calculate the number of years the bond was held.
solution_steps: The par value is \(F=1000\).
The coupon rate is \(9\%\) payable semiannually, so the coupon rate per half-year is \(r=0.09/2=0.045\).
Thus, each semiannual coupon payment is \(Fr=1000(0.045)=45\).
The bond was called for \(C=1100\).
The nominal yield rate convertible semiannually is \(10\%\), so the yield rate per half-year is \(i=0.10/2=0.05\).
Let \(n\) be the number of half-years the bond was held.
Since the bond was bought immediately after a coupon payment, the first coupon received is one half-year later.
The value equation is \(917.44=45a_{\overline{n}|0.05}+1100v_{0.05}^n\).
Solving this equation gives \(n=50\).
Since \(n\) is measured in half-years, the number of years is \(50/2=25\).
generalization: When a bond is bought immediately after a coupon payment and held until call, let \(n\) be the number of coupon periods until the call date. The purchase price equals the present value of the coupons received plus the present value of the call price.
answer_text: The bond was held for \(\boxed{25}\) years.

title: Exercise 209
statement: A 1000 par value bond pays annual coupons of 80. The bond is redeemable at par in 30 years, but is callable any time from the end of the 10th year at 1050. Based on her desired yield rate, an investor calculates the following potential purchase prices P: (i) Assuming the bond is called at the end of the 10th year, P = 957. (ii) Assuming the bond is held until maturity, P = 897. The investor buys the bond at the highest price that guarantees she will receive at least her desired yield rate regardless of when the bond is called. The investor holds the bond for 20 years, after which time the bond is called. Calculate the annual yield rate the investor earns.
solution_steps: For a callable bond, the investor must choose the highest price that guarantees at least the desired yield regardless of when the bond is called.
This means we choose the minimum of the possible purchase prices.
The two possible purchase prices are \(957\) and \(897\).
Therefore, the investor pays \(P=897\).
The bond is held for 20 years and then called for \(1050\).
The annual coupon payment is \(Fr=80\).
Let \(i\) be the annual effective yield rate actually earned.
The value equation is \(897=80a_{\overline{20}|i}+1050v_i^{20}\).
Solving for \(i\) gives \(i=0.0924\).
Therefore, the annual yield rate earned is \(9.24\%\).
generalization: For a callable bond, the purchase price that guarantees a minimum yield is the minimum of the prices calculated over all possible redemption dates. Once the actual call date is known, the realized yield is found by solving the value equation using the actual holding period and call price.
answer_text: The annual yield rate earned by the investor is \(\boxed{9.24\%}\).

title: Exercise 210
statement: An investor purchases a 1000 bond redeemable at par that pays 8% semiannual coupons and matures in 10 years. The bond will yield 7% convertible semiannually to maturity. If the bond is called in five years, the minimum redemption value the investor needs to realize the same yield is X. Determine X.
solution_steps: A 1000 bond means that the par value is \(F=1000\).
The bond is redeemable at par, so the redemption value at maturity is \(C=1000\).
The bond pays \(8\%\) semiannual coupons, so the coupon rate per half-year is \(r=0.08/2=0.04\).
Thus, each semiannual coupon payment is \(Fr=1000(0.04)=40\).
The yield rate is \(7\%\) convertible semiannually, so the yield rate per half-year is \(i=0.07/2=0.035\).
Since the bond matures in 10 years and pays semiannual coupons, the number of coupon periods is \(n=10(2)=20\).
First, find the original purchase price of the bond: \(P=40a_{\overline{20}|0.035}+1000v_{0.035}^{20}\).
Equivalently, using the premium / discount formula, \(P=C+(Fr-Ci)a_{\overline{20}|i}\).
If the bond is called in five years, then there are \(5(2)=10\) semiannual periods.
To realize the same yield, we use the same semiannual yield rate \(i=0.035\).
The value equation is \(1071.06=40a_{\overline{10}|0.035}+Xv_{0.035}^{10}\).
The closest answer is \(1042\).
generalization: When a bond is called earlier than maturity, use the original purchase price and the desired yield rate to solve for the call price that gives the investor the same yield over the shorter holding period.
answer_text: The minimum redemption value is approximately \(\boxed{1041.58}\), so the closest answer is B: \(1042\).

title: Exercise 211
statement: A 1000 ten-year bond bearing annual coupons of 4% is callable at par at any time after the fifth year. A purchaser pays a price for the bond that will assure him of a yield of not less than 5% if it is held to maturity or prior call. Find: (i) the purchase price to the nearest dollar; (ii) the call date most favorable to the purchaser.
choices: Purchase price: \(923\); Call date: 5 years hence
Purchase price: \(923\); Call date: 10 years hence
Purchase price: \(957\); Call date: 5 years hence
Purchase price: \(957\); Call date: 10 years hence
Purchase price: \(1000\); Call date: 10 years hence
solution_steps: The par value is \(F=1000\).
The bond pays annual coupons of \(4\%\), so the annual coupon payment is \(Fr=1000(0.04)=40\).
The desired yield rate is \(i=0.05\).
The bond is callable at par, so the redemption value is \(C=1000\).
If the bond is called or redeemed at time \(n\), its price at a \(5\%\) yield is \(P=40a_{\overline{n}|0.05}+1000v_{0.05}^n\).
Using the premium / discount formula, \(P=C+(Fr-Ci)a_{\overline{n}|i}\).
Therefore, \(P=1000-10a_{\overline{n}|0.05}\).
To guarantee a yield of at least \(5\%\), the purchaser must use the minimum of the possible prices.
Therefore, the purchase price is \(923\).
If the purchaser pays \(923\), the most favorable call date for the purchaser is the earliest possible call date, which is 5 years hence.
This is because the bond is bought at a discount. Receiving the par value earlier gives the investor a higher realized yield.
generalization: For a callable bond, the purchase price that guarantees a minimum yield is the minimum of the prices computed over all possible redemption dates. After the investor pays that safe price, the most favorable call date is the date that gives the highest realized yield.
answer_text: The purchase price is \(\boxed{923}\), and the call date most favorable to the purchaser is \(\boxed{5\text{ years hence}}\). The correct answer is A.

title: Exercise 212
statement: A 1000 par-value 4% bond with semiannual coupons matures at the end of 10 years. The bond is callable at 1050 on any coupon date from the 3rd through the 6th years, at 1025 from the 7th through the 9th years, and at 1000 during the 10th year. Find the maximum price that an investor can pay and still be certain of a yield of 5% convertible semiannually.
solution_steps: The par value is \(F=1000\).
The bond pays \(4\%\) semiannual coupons, so the coupon rate per half-year is \(r=0.04/2=0.02\).
Thus, each semiannual coupon payment is \(Fr=1000(0.02)=20\).
The required yield is \(5\%\) convertible semiannually, so the yield rate per half-year is \(i=0.05/2=0.025\).
The maximum price that guarantees the required yield is the minimum price over all possible call dates.
For a call date \(n\) semiannual periods from now, the price is \(P=20a_{\overline{n}|0.025}+Cv_{0.025}^n\), where \(C\) is the call price at that time.
For call dates from the 3rd through the 6th years, \(C=1050\), so \(P=20a_{\overline{n}|0.025}+1050v_{0.025}^n\), where \(n=5,\ldots,12\).
For call dates from the 7th through the 9th years, \(C=1025\), so \(P=20a_{\overline{n}|0.025}+1025v_{0.025}^n\), where \(n=13,\ldots,18\).
The smallest of these prices is \(922\).
Therefore, the maximum price the investor can pay and still be certain of a yield of \(5\%\) convertible semiannually is \(922\).
generalization: For a callable bond, the investor must calculate the price at each possible call date using the required yield and then choose the minimum price. This ensures the required yield is achieved regardless of when the bond is called.
answer_text: The maximum price is \(\boxed{922}\), so the correct answer is A.

title: Exercise 213
statement: What is the gist of the phenomenon called term structure of interest rates?
correct: Interest rates vary by the term of the investment.
solution_steps: The term structure of interest rates describes how interest rates depend on the term, or length, of the investment.
Otherwise similar investments may have different yield rates if they have different maturities.
Therefore, the main idea is that interest rates vary by the term of the investment.
generalization: The term structure of interest rates is represented graphically by the yield curve, which shows the relationship between maturity and yield rate.
answer_text: Interest rates vary by the term of the investment.

title: Exercise 214
statement: Company XYZ invests 10000 into a project that expects to return 5000 one year after the investment and 7000 two years after the investment. For XYZ's investment, the one-year spot rate is 10%. Calculate the two-year spot rate.
solution_steps: Let \(s_2\) be the two-year spot rate.
The initial investment is \(10000\).
The project returns \(5000\) at time 1 and \(7000\) at time 2.
The one-year spot rate is \(s_1=0.10\).
Using spot rates, the present value equation is \(10000=\frac{5000}{1.10}+\frac{7000}{(1+s_2)^2}\).
Taking the square root gives \(1+s_2=\sqrt{1.28333}\).
generalization: When using spot rates, each cash flow is discounted using the spot rate corresponding to its timing. A cash flow at time 1 uses \(s_1\), while a cash flow at time 2 uses \(s_2\).
answer_text: The two-year spot rate is \(\boxed{13.3\%}\).

title: Exercise 215
statement: Given the following annual effective spot rates: s_1 = 4.00%, s_2 = 4.50%, s_3 = 4.75%, and s_4 = 5.00%. Calculate 100(Ia)_{\overline{4}|}.
solution_steps: The expression \(100(Ia)_{\overline{4}|}\) represents an increasing annuity-immediate with payments \(100, 200, 300, 400\) at times \(1,2,3,4\), respectively.
Since spot rates are given, each cash flow must be discounted using the spot rate corresponding to its payment time.
The payment of \(100\) at time \(1\) is discounted using \(s_1=4.00\%\), giving \(100/1.04\).
The payment of \(200\) at time \(2\) is discounted using \(s_2=4.50\%\), giving \(200/(1.045)^2\).
The payment of \(300\) at time \(3\) is discounted using \(s_3=4.75\%\), giving \(300/(1.0475)^3\).
The payment of \(400\) at time \(4\) is discounted using \(s_4=5.00\%\), giving \(400/(1.05)^4\).
This gives \(100(Ia)_{\overline{4}|}=869.39\).
generalization: When spot rates are given, each cash flow is discounted using the spot rate corresponding to its time. For an increasing annuity-immediate with payments \(100,200,\ldots,100n\), the present value is the sum of each payment discounted by its own spot rate.
answer_text: The value of \(100(Ia)_{\overline{4}|}\) is \(\boxed{869.39}\).

title: Exercise 216
statement: Given the following prices of zero-coupon bonds with a redemption amount of 105: maturity 1 price 100.96, maturity 2 price 98.02, maturity 3 price 95.39, maturity 4 price 93.29. Calculate the price of a 4-year 100 par value bond with 5% annual coupons.
solution_steps: The bond has par value \(F=100\).
The annual coupon rate is \(5\%\), so each annual coupon is \(100(0.05)=5\).
The bond matures in 4 years, so the cash flows are \(5\) at time 1, \(5\) at time 2, \(5\) at time 3, and \(105\) at time 4.
We are given prices of zero-coupon bonds that each pay \(105\) at maturity.
Using the percentage of price method, the present value of a cash flow at the same time is proportional to the price of the corresponding zero-coupon bond.
The present value of the time 1 coupon is \(100.96(5/105)\).
The present value of the time 2 coupon is \(98.02(5/105)\).
The present value of the time 3 coupon is \(95.39(5/105)\).
The present value of the time 4 cash flow is \(93.29(105/105)\).
This gives \(P=107.31\).
generalization: When zero-coupon bond prices are given, the percentage of price method can be used to value cash flows at the same maturity. If a zero-coupon bond paying \(A\) at time \(t\) has price \(P_A\), then the present value of \(B\) at time \(t\) is \(P_A(B/A)\).
answer_text: The price of the bond is \(\boxed{107.31}\).

title: Exercise 217
statement: Consider the following information on three U.S. Treasury bonds with coupons paid semiannually: Bond A has principal 100, maturity 0.50 years, annual coupon 0, and price 95.00. Bond B has principal 100, maturity 1.00 year, annual coupon 9, and price 98.50. Bond C has principal 100, maturity 1.50 years, annual coupon 12, and price 101.50. Using the bootstrap method, calculate the continuously compounded 1.5-year zero rate.
solution_steps: Let \(\delta_{0.5}\), \(\delta_1\), and \(\delta_{1.5}\) denote the continuously compounded zero rates for maturities \(0.5\), \(1\), and \(1.5\) years.
Since coupons are paid semiannually, the semiannual coupon payment is one-half of the annual coupon.
Bond A has no coupon and matures in \(0.5\) years, so \(100e^{-0.5\delta_{0.5}}=95\).
Solving gives \(\delta_{0.5}=0.1026\).
Bond B has an annual coupon of \(9\), so its semiannual coupon is \(9/2=4.5\).
Bond B has cash flows \(4.5\) at time \(0.5\) and \(104.5\) at time \(1\).
Using \(\delta_{0.5}=0.1026\), solving gives \(\delta_1=0.1035\).
Bond C has an annual coupon of \(12\), so its semiannual coupon is \(12/2=6\).
Bond C has cash flows \(6\) at time \(0.5\), \(6\) at time \(1\), and \(106\) at time \(1.5\).
Substituting the previously found zero rates gives \(6e^{-0.5(0.1026)}+6e^{-0.1035}+106e^{-1.5\delta_{1.5}}=101.50\).
Solving gives \(\delta_{1.5}=0.1062\).
generalization: Bootstrapping means solving for zero rates sequentially. First solve for the shortest maturity zero rate, then use it to solve for the next maturity, and continue until the desired maturity is reached.
answer_text: The continuously compounded 1.5-year zero rate is \(\boxed{0.1062}\), so the correct answer is B.

title: Exercise 218
statement: You are given the following information with respect to a bond: par value 1000, term to maturity 3 years, and annual coupon rate 6% payable annually. You are also given that the one-year, two-year, and three-year annual spot interest rates are 7%, 8%, and 9%, respectively. Calculate the value of the bond.
solution_steps: The par value is \(F=1000\).
The annual coupon rate is \(6\%\), so the annual coupon payment is \(1000(0.06)=60\).
Since the bond matures in 3 years and coupons are paid annually, the cash flows are \(60\) at time 1, \(60\) at time 2, and \(1060\) at time 3.
Using spot rates, each cash flow must be discounted using the spot rate corresponding to its payment time.
The one-year spot rate is \(s_1=7\%\), the two-year spot rate is \(s_2=8\%\), and the three-year spot rate is \(s_3=9\%\).
Therefore, the bond value is \(P=\frac{60}{1.07}+\frac{60}{(1.08)^2}+\frac{1060}{(1.09)^3}\).
This gives \(P=926.03\).
The closest answer is \(926\).
generalization: When spot rates are given, each cash flow is discounted using the spot rate corresponding to its payment time. A time 1 cash flow uses \(s_1\), a time 2 cash flow uses \(s_2\), and so on.
answer_text: The value of the bond is approximately \(\boxed{926.03}\), so the correct answer is B.

title: Exercise 219
statement: Given the following spot rates: s_1 = 4.00%, s_2 = 5.00%, s_3 = 5.50%, s_4 = 5.75%, and s_5 = 6.00%. Calculate the 3-year forward rate, deferred for two years.
solution_steps: A 3-year forward rate deferred for two years is the rate from time 2 to time 5.
Therefore, we want \(f_{2,5}\).
There are two equivalent ways to accumulate \(1\) from time \(0\) to time \(5\).
Using the 5-year spot rate gives \((1+s_5)^5\).
Using the 2-year spot rate first and then the 3-year forward rate gives \((1+s_2)^2(1+f_{2,5})^3\).
Taking the cube root gives \(1+f_{2,5}=\left[\frac{(1.06)^5}{(1.05)^2}\right]^{1/3}\).
generalization: An m-year forward rate deferred t years is found by equating accumulation from time 0 to time t+m using spot rates and forward rates: \((1+s_t)^t(1+f_{t,t+m})^m=(1+s_{t+m})^{t+m}\).
answer_text: The 3-year forward rate deferred for two years is \(\boxed{6.672\%}\).

title: Exercise 220
statement: You are given the following information about the Treasury market. Treasuries always have a par value of 100. A 1-year zero-coupon Treasury has price 96.62, a 2-year zero-coupon Treasury has price x, and a 3-year zero-coupon Treasury has price 88.90. It is known that the one-year forward rate starting in two years is 4.5%. Calculate x.
solution_steps: Since the Treasuries have \(0\%\) coupons, they are zero-coupon bonds.
The 3-year Treasury has price \(88.90\) and pays \(100\) at time \(3\).
The one-year forward rate starting in two years is \(f_{2,3}=0.045\).
Let \(s_2\) be the 2-year spot rate.
Using the 2-year spot rate from time \(0\) to time \(2\), and then the forward rate from time \(2\) to time \(3\), we get \(88.90=\frac{100}{(1+s_2)^2(1.045)}\).
Taking the square root gives \(1+s_2=\sqrt{1.0764}\).
The 2-year Treasury price is \(x=\frac{100}{(1+s_2)^2}\).
generalization: If a forward rate \(f_{t,t+m}\) is known, it can be combined with a spot rate to price a zero-coupon bond. The key relationship is \((1+s_t)^t(1+f_{t,t+m})^m=(1+s_{t+m})^{t+m}\).
answer_text: The price of the 2-year Treasury is \(\boxed{92.90}\).

title: Exercise 220
statement: You are given the following information about the Treasury market. Treasuries always have a par value of 100. A 1-year zero-coupon Treasury has price 96.62, a 2-year zero-coupon Treasury has price x, and a 3-year zero-coupon Treasury has price 88.90. It is known that the one-year forward rate starting in two years is 4.5%. Calculate x.
solution_steps: Since the Treasuries have \(0\%\) coupons, they are zero-coupon bonds.
The 3-year Treasury has price \(88.90\) and pays \(100\) at time \(3\).
The one-year forward rate starting in two years is \(f_{2,3}=0.045\).
Let \(s_2\) be the 2-year spot rate.
Using the 2-year spot rate from time \(0\) to time \(2\), and then the forward rate from time \(2\) to time \(3\), we get \(88.90=\frac{100}{(1+s_2)^2(1.045)}\).
Taking the square root gives \(1+s_2=\sqrt{1.0764}\).
The 2-year Treasury price is \(x=\frac{100}{(1+s_2)^2}\).
generalization: If a forward rate \(f_{t,t+m}\) is known, it can be combined with a spot rate to price a zero-coupon bond. The key relationship is \((1+s_t)^t(1+f_{t,t+m})^m=(1+s_{t+m})^{t+m}\).
answer_text: The price of the 2-year Treasury is \(\boxed{92.90}\).

title: Exercise 221
statement: You are given: (i) the one-year spot rate is 5%, (ii) the one-year forward rate starting in one year is 5.5%, and (iii) the one-year forward rate starting in two years is 6.0%. Calculate \(\ddot{a}_{\overline{3}|}\).
solution_steps: An annuity-due with 3 payments has payments of \(1\) at times \(0,1,2\).
Therefore, \(\ddot{a}_{\overline{3}|}=1+PV(1\text{ at time }1)+PV(1\text{ at time }2)\).
The payment at time \(0\) is already at the valuation date, so its present value is \(1\).
The payment at time \(1\) is discounted using the one-year spot rate, giving \(1/1.05\).
The payment at time \(2\) is discounted using the one-year spot rate for year 1 and the one-year forward rate for year 2, giving \(1/[(1.05)(1.055)]\).
This gives \(\ddot{a}_{\overline{3}|}=2.855\).
generalization: When valuing an annuity using forward rates, discount each payment by the product of the one-year rates that apply before that payment date. For an annuity-due, remember that the first payment occurs immediately at time 0.
answer_text: The value is \(\boxed{2.855}\).

title: Exercise 222
statement: You are given the following information about three bonds. Bond A has price 96 on Jan. 1, 2001 and cash inflow 100 on Jul. 1, 2001. Bond B has price 88 on Jan. 1, 2001 and cash inflow 100 on Jul. 1, 2002. Bond C has price 125 on Jan. 1, 2001 and cash inflow 150 on Jan. 1, 2003. Assuming no arbitrage opportunity exists, calculate the forward rate from July 1, 2002 to January 1, 2003 as a nominal rate convertible semiannually.
solution_steps: Set January 1, 2001 as time \(0\).
Then July 1, 2002 is time \(1.5\), and January 1, 2003 is time \(2\).
We need the forward rate from time \(1.5\) to time \(2\), expressed as a nominal rate convertible semiannually.
Bond B costs \(88\) at time \(0\) and pays \(100\) at time \(1.5\), so the accumulation factor from time \(0\) to time \(1.5\) is \(a(1.5)=100/88\).
Bond C costs \(125\) at time \(0\) and pays \(150\) at time \(2\), so the accumulation factor from time \(0\) to time \(2\) is \(a(2)=150/125\).
The effective forward rate for the half-year from time \(1.5\) to time \(2\) is \(a(2)/a(1.5)-1\).
Thus, the effective half-year forward rate is \(1.056-1=0.056\).
Since the answer is requested as a nominal rate convertible semiannually, multiply by \(2\): \(i^{(2)}=2(0.056)=0.112\).
generalization: To find a forward rate between two future dates, compare the accumulation factors to the two dates. If the forward rate is for one half-year and the answer is requested as a nominal rate convertible semiannually, double the effective half-year rate.
answer_text: The forward rate is \(\boxed{0.112}\), so the correct answer is E.

title: Exercise 223
statement: The following is a list of prices for zero-coupon bonds with par value of 1000 and varying maturities: maturity 1 price 943.40, maturity 2 price 898.47, maturity 3 price 847.62, and maturity 4 price 792.16. Calculate the 1-year forward rate, deferred 3 years.
solution_steps: We are looking for the 1-year forward rate deferred 3 years. This is the forward rate from year \(3\) to year \(4\).
Let this forward rate be \(f_{3,4}\).
Since the bonds are zero-coupon bonds with par value \(1000\), the accumulation factor from time \(0\) to time \(3\) is \(1000/847.62\).
The accumulation factor from time \(0\) to time \(4\) is \(1000/792.16\).
The 4-year accumulation factor must equal the 3-year accumulation factor multiplied by the 1-year forward accumulation factor from year \(3\) to year \(4\).
generalization: For zero-coupon bonds, the price gives the discount factor directly. To find a forward rate between years \(t\) and \(t+1\), compare the accumulation factors to time \(t\) and time \(t+1\).
answer_text: The 1-year forward rate deferred 3 years is \(\boxed{0.070}\), so the correct answer is C.

title: Exercise 224
statement: You are given the following information with respect to a non-callable bond: par amount 1000, term to maturity 4 years, and annual coupon rate 8% payable annually. The one-year annual forward interest rates are given under two equally likely scenarios. Scenario X has rates 7%, 7%, 8%, and 10% at times 0, 1, 2, and 3. Scenario Y has rates 7%, 6%, 7%, and 5% at times 0, 1, 2, and 3. Calculate the value of the bond, that is, the expected present value of the bond payments.
solution_steps: The annual coupon payment is \(1000(0.08)=80\).
Since the bond matures in 4 years, the cash flows are \(80\), \(80\), \(80\), and \(1080\).
We discount the cash flows separately under each interest rate scenario, then average the two present values.
Under Scenario X, the one-year forward rates are \(7\%\), \(7\%\), \(8\%\), and \(10\%\).
This gives \(PV_X=1003.37\).
Under Scenario Y, the one-year forward rates are \(7\%\), \(6\%\), \(7\%\), and \(5\%\).
This gives \(PV_Y=1058.76\).
Since the two scenarios are equally likely, the expected present value is \((PV_X+PV_Y)/2\).
generalization: When future one-year forward rates are scenario-dependent, calculate the present value of the bond cash flows under each scenario, then take the probability-weighted average of those present values.
answer_text: The value of the bond is \(\boxed{1031.07}\), so the correct answer is E.

title: Exercise 225
statement: The current real rate of interest is 4%. The expected annual inflation rate over the next several years is 5%. Given the following expected nominal cash flows: at year 0, -300; at year 1, 160; at year 2, 160. What is the net present value of the investment using the nominal rate of interest?
solution_steps: Let \(i'\) be the real rate of interest, \(r\) the inflation rate, and \(i\) the nominal rate of interest.
We are given \(i'=0.04\) and \(r=0.05\).
The relationship between the nominal rate, inflation rate, and real rate is \(1+i=(1+r)(1+i')\).
Substitute the given values: \(1+i=(1.05)(1.04)\).
Therefore, the nominal rate of interest is \(9.2\%\).
Since the cash flows are nominal cash flows, discount them using the nominal rate \(i=0.092\).
The net present value is \(NPV=-300+\frac{160}{1.092}+\frac{160}{(1.092)^2}\).
This gives \(NPV=-19.30\).
generalization: When cash flows are nominal, discount them using the nominal interest rate. If the real rate \(i'\) and inflation rate \(r\) are given, use \(1+i=(1+r)(1+i')\) to find the nominal rate.
answer_text: The net present value is \(\boxed{-19.30}\).

topic: Price as a Function of Yield
title: Exercise 226
statement: True or false: when interest rates decrease, bond prices go down.
solution_steps: The statement is false.
Bond prices and interest rates are inversely related.
The price of a bond is the present value of its future cash flows: \(P=\sum_t CF_t(1+i)^{-t}\).
If the interest rate \(i\) decreases, then the discount factor \((1+i)^{-t}\) increases.
Therefore, the present value of the bond's cash flows increases.
So when interest rates decrease, bond prices go up, not down.
generalization: Bond prices and yield rates move in opposite directions. When yields increase, prices decrease. When yields decrease, prices increase.
answer_text: The statement is false.

title: Exercise 227
statement: Which of the following bonds is the most price sensitive to changes in interest rates?
solution_steps: Cash flows further into the future are more sensitive to changes in interest rates.
A zero-coupon bond has only one cash flow, paid at maturity.
Therefore, the longer the maturity, the more sensitive the bond price is to changes in interest rates.
Among the choices, the longest maturity is the 20-year zero-coupon bond.
Thus, the 20-year zero-coupon bond is the most price sensitive.
generalization: For zero-coupon bonds, price sensitivity increases with maturity. Longer maturity means the single cash flow is further in the future, so its present value reacts more strongly to yield changes.
answer_text: The most price sensitive bond is the 20-year zero-coupon bond. The correct answer is C.

title: Exercise 228
statement: Let x be the price change for a 20-year zero-coupon bond when interest rates change from 5% to 4%. Let y be the price change for the same bond when interest rates change from 15% to 14%. True or false: |x| > |y|.
solution_steps: The statement is true.
The same 20-year zero-coupon bond is used in both cases.
The yield decreases by one percentage point in both cases.
However, bond prices are more sensitive to interest rate changes when the current yield rate is lower.
The change from 5% to 4% occurs at a lower yield level than the change from 15% to 14%.
Therefore, the absolute price change is larger for the change from 5% to 4%.
generalization: Price sensitivity depends not only on the size of the yield change but also on the current yield level. For the same change in yield, bond prices are generally more sensitive when yields are lower.
answer_text: The statement is true.

title: Exercise 229
