statement: Infinite Life's only liability is due in 5 years. The liability is 5000. Calculate the price sensitivity of the liability for an increase in yield from an annual effective rate of 6% to 7%.
solution_steps: The liability is a single payment of \(5000\) due in 5 years.
At yield \(6\%\), the price of the liability is \(P_{0.06}=5000v_{0.06}^{5}\).
At yield \(7\%\), the price of the liability is \(P_{0.07}=5000v_{0.07}^{5}\).
Price sensitivity is the percentage change in price: \(\text{Price Sensitivity}=\frac{P_{0.07}-P_{0.06}}{P_{0.06}}\).
Therefore, \(\text{Price Sensitivity}=\frac{3564.93-3736.29}{3736.29}\).
This gives \(\text{Price Sensitivity}=-0.0459\).
generalization: Price sensitivity measures the percentage change in the present value of a cash flow when the yield changes. When the yield increases, the present value decreases, so the price sensitivity is negative.
answer_text: The price sensitivity is \(\boxed{-0.0459}\), so the correct answer is B.

title: Exercise 230
statement: A 20-year bond pays semiannual coupons of 7.4% and is priced at par. Calculate the duration.
solution_steps: Since the bond is priced at par, we can use the shortcut for the Macaulay duration of a par bond.
The bond has semiannual coupons, so \(m=2\).
The term is 20 years, so the total number of coupon periods is \(20(2)=40\).
The semiannual yield rate is \(j=0.074/2=0.037\).
For an \(n\)-year par bond with \(m\)-thly coupons, the Macaulay duration is \(\text{MacD}=\frac{1}{m}\ddot{a}_{\overline{mn}|j}\).
Therefore, \(\text{MacD}=\frac{1}{2}\ddot{a}_{\overline{40}|0.037}\).
This gives \(\text{MacD}=10.737\).
generalization: For a par bond, the Macaulay duration has a shortcut: \(\text{MacD}=\frac{1}{m}\ddot{a}_{\overline{mn}|j}\), where \(m\) is the number of coupon payments per year and \(j\) is the yield rate per coupon period.
answer_text: The duration is \(\boxed{10.737\text{ years}}\).

title: Exercise 231
statement: A three-year bond with 8% annual coupons has a redemption value of 105% of the par value and is priced to yield an annual effective rate of 6%. Calculate the duration.
solution_steps: The bond is not priced at par, so we cannot use the par bond duration shortcut.
Since duration is unaffected by scaling, let the par value be \(F=1\).
The annual coupon is \(0.08\).
The redemption value is \(1.05\).
Therefore, the cash flows are \(0.08\) at time 1, \(0.08\) at time 2, and \(0.08+1.05=1.13\) at time 3.
The annual effective yield rate is \(i=0.06\), so \(v=1/1.06\).
The Macaulay duration is \(\text{MacD}=\frac{\sum tA_t}{\sum A_t}\), where \(A_t\) is the present value of the cash flow at time \(t\).
Substituting \(v=v_{0.06}\), we get \(\text{MacD}=2.7972\).
generalization: When a bond is not priced at par, do not use the par bond shortcut for Macaulay duration. Instead, calculate duration directly as the present-value-weighted average time of the cash flows.
answer_text: The duration is \(\boxed{2.7972\text{ years}}\).

title: Exercise 232
statement: An annuity-immediate makes monthly payments of 120 for 15 years. The constant force of interest is 0.05. Calculate the duration of the annuity.
solution_steps: The annuity makes monthly payments for 15 years, so the total number of payments is \(12(15)=180\).
Since the force of interest is constant with \(\delta=0.05\), the effective monthly interest rate is \(j=e^{0.05/12}-1\).
The first payment occurs at time \(1/12\), the second at time \(2/12\), and so on.
For a level annuity-immediate with \(m\) payments per year, the Macaulay duration is \(\text{MacD}=\frac{(Ia)_{\overline{mn}|j}}{m a_{\overline{mn}|j}}\).
Thus, \(\text{MacD}=\frac{(Ia)_{\overline{180}|j}}{12a_{\overline{180}|j}}\).
Equivalently, using the payment amount \(120\), \(\text{MacD}=\frac{10(Ia)_{\overline{180}|j}}{120a_{\overline{180}|j}}\), because \(120(k/12)=10k\).
Therefore, \(\text{MacD}=6.613\).
generalization: For a level annuity-immediate with payments made \(m\) times per year, the Macaulay duration is \(\text{MacD}=\frac{(Ia)_{\overline{mn}|j}}{m a_{\overline{mn}|j}}\), where \(j\) is the effective interest rate per payment period.
answer_text: The duration of the annuity is \(\boxed{6.613\text{ years}}\).

title: Exercise 233
statement: You are given a 1000 par value 20-year bond with 4% annual coupons and a maturity value of 1000. Calculate the duration of this bond at 5% interest.
solution_steps: The annual coupon payment is \(Fr=1000(0.04)=40\).
The maturity value is \(C=1000\).
The annual effective yield rate is \(i=0.05\), so \(v=1/1.05\).
Since the coupon rate is \(4\%\) but the yield rate is \(5\%\), the bond is not priced at par. Therefore, we cannot use the par bond shortcut.
We use the general Macaulay duration formula \(\text{MacD}=\frac{\sum tA_t}{P}\), where \(A_t\) is the present value of the cash flow at time \(t\).
The numerator of the Macaulay duration is \(40(Ia)_{\overline{20}|0.05}+20(1000)v_{0.05}^{20}\).
This gives \(\text{MacD}=13.68\).
Rounded to one decimal place, \(\text{MacD}=13.7\).
generalization: When a bond is not priced at par, do not use the par bond duration shortcut. Instead, calculate Macaulay duration directly as the present-value-weighted average time of the bond cash flows.
answer_text: The duration is approximately \(\boxed{13.7}\), so the correct answer is C.

title: Exercise 234
statement: A perpetuity-due with level payments has a duration of 10 at an effective interest rate of i. Calculate i.
solution_steps: Assume the level payment is \(1\). Since this is a perpetuity-due, payments occur at times \(0,1,2,3,\ldots\).
The Macaulay duration is \(\text{MacD}=\frac{\sum tA_t}{\sum A_t}\), where \(A_t\) is the present value of the cash flow at time \(t\).
The denominator is the present value of a perpetuity-due: \(\sum A_t=\ddot{a}_{\overline{\infty}|}=1+\frac{1}{i}\).
The numerator is the time-weighted present value of the cash flows: \(0(1)+1v+2v^2+3v^3+\cdots\).
This is the present value of an increasing perpetuity-immediate: \((Ia)_{\overline{\infty}|}=\frac{1}{i}+\frac{1}{i^2}\).
Therefore, \(\text{MacD}=\frac{(Ia)_{\overline{\infty}|}}{\ddot{a}_{\overline{\infty}|}}=\frac{\frac{1}{i}+\frac{1}{i^2}}{1+\frac{1}{i}}\).
Simplifying gives \(\text{MacD}=\frac{1}{i}\).
Since the duration is \(10\), we have \(10=\frac{1}{i}\).
generalization: For a level perpetuity-due, the Macaulay duration is \(1/i\). Therefore, if the duration is known, the effective interest rate is the reciprocal of the duration.
answer_text: The effective interest rate is \(\boxed{10\%}\), so the correct answer is D.

title: Exercise 235
statement: Kylie bought a 7-year, 5000 par value bond with an annual coupon rate of 7.6% paid semiannually. She bought the bond with no premium or discount. Calculate the Macaulay duration of this bond with respect to the yield rate on the bond.
solution_steps: Since Kylie bought the bond with no premium or discount, the bond is priced at par.
Therefore, we can use the shortcut for the Macaulay duration of a bond priced at par.
The bond pays coupons semiannually, so \(m=2\).
The bond has a term of 7 years, so the total number of coupon periods is \(7(2)=14\).
The annual coupon rate is \(7.6\%\), paid semiannually. Since the bond is priced at par, the yield rate per six-month period is \(j=0.076/2=0.038\).
For a par bond with semiannual coupons, the Macaulay duration is \(\text{MacD}=\frac{1}{2}\ddot{a}_{\overline{14}|0.038}\).
This gives \(\text{MacD}=5.555\).
Rounded to two decimal places, \(\text{MacD}=5.56\).
Alternatively, without the shortcut, the semiannual coupon payment is \(Fr=5000(0.076/2)=190\).
Using the general formula, \(\text{MacD}=\frac{\sum tA_t}{P}\).
Because time is measured in years, the \(k\)-th coupon is paid at time \(k/2\).
Since the bond is priced at par, the denominator is \(5000\).
generalization: When a bond is bought with no premium or discount, it is priced at par. For a par bond with \(m\) coupon payments per year, the Macaulay duration shortcut is \(\text{MacD}=\frac{1}{m}\ddot{a}_{\overline{mn}|j}\), where \(j\) is the yield rate per coupon period.
answer_text: The Macaulay duration is \(\boxed{5.56}\), so the correct answer is C.

title: Exercise 236
statement: A five-year bond with annual coupons of 100 and a redemption amount of 1500 is priced at 1608.24 to yield 5%. Your coworker uses the bond's modified duration to estimate the change in price if the yield rises to 6%. The coworker's estimated change in price is 76.58. Determine the estimated price of the bond at a 6% yield. Is this estimated price larger or smaller than the actual price at 6%?
solution_steps: An increase in yield means a decrease in the bond price.
The estimated change in price is \(76.58\), so the estimated price is \(1608.24-76.58\).
Thus, the estimated price is \(1531.66\).
Now calculate the actual price at a \(6\%\) yield.
The bond pays annual coupons of \(100\) for 5 years and a redemption amount of \(1500\) at maturity.
The modified duration estimate is \(1531.66\), while the actual price is \(1542.12\).
Therefore, the estimated price is smaller than the actual price.
generalization: Modified duration uses a tangent-line approximation to estimate price changes. For bonds with positive convexity, the modified duration estimate tends to underestimate the actual price, especially when the yield change is large.
answer_text: The estimated price is \(\boxed{1531.66}\). This is smaller than the actual price at a 6% yield, which is \(1542.12\).

title: Exercise 237
statement: A five-year bond with annual coupons of 100 and a redemption amount of 1500 is priced at 1608.24 to yield 5%. Your coworker uses the bond's modified duration to estimate the change in price if the yield rises to 6%. The coworker's estimated change in price is 76.58. Determine the modified duration of the bond using the effective annual yield.
solution_steps: The modified duration approximation is \(\%\Delta P \approx -\Delta i \cdot \text{ModD}\).
The yield rises from \(5\%\) to \(6\%\), so \(\Delta i=0.06-0.05=0.01\).
Since the yield increases, the price decreases. Therefore, the estimated change in price is \(\Delta P=-76.58\).
The percentage change in price is \(\%\Delta P=\frac{\Delta P}{P_{0.05}}=\frac{-76.58}{1608.24}\).
Thus, \(\frac{-76.58}{1608.24}\approx -0.01\cdot \text{ModD}\).
Solving for modified duration gives \(\text{ModD}=\frac{76.58}{1608.24(0.01)}\).
Therefore, \(\text{ModD}=4.762\).
generalization: Modified duration can be recovered from an estimated dollar price change by first converting the dollar change into a percentage price change. Then use \(\%\Delta P \approx -\Delta i\cdot\text{ModD}\).
answer_text: The modified duration of the bond is \(\boxed{4.762}\).

title: Exercise 238
statement: Which of the following are true? (i) Bond prices and yields are inversely related. (ii) Cash flows further into the future are more sensitive to changes in interest rates. (iii) Modified duration is a measure of price sensitivity.
choices: The correct answer is not given by A, B, C, or D
solution_steps: Statement 1 is true. Bond prices and yields are inversely related.
The price of a bond is the present value of its future cash flows: \(P=\sum_t CF_t(1+i)^{-t}\).
As the yield rate \(i\) increases, the discount factors \((1+i)^{-t}\) decrease, so the bond price decreases.
Statement 2 is true. A cash flow further in the future is more sensitive to changes in interest rates because it is discounted over a longer period.
For a single cash flow, \(PV=CF(1+i)^{-t}\). The larger \(t\) is, the more strongly a change in \(i\) affects the present value.
Statement 3 is true. Modified duration is used to estimate the percentage change in price caused by a change in yield.
The modified duration approximation is \(\%\Delta P \approx -\Delta i \cdot \text{ModD}\).
Thus, modified duration is a measure of price sensitivity.
Therefore, statements 1, 2, and 3 are all true.
generalization: Bond prices and yields move in opposite directions. Longer-dated cash flows are more sensitive to interest rate changes, and modified duration summarizes this price sensitivity.
answer_text: Statements 1, 2, and 3 are all true, so the correct answer is D.

title: Exercise 239
statement: The modified duration of a bond is 6. Given a yield rate of 4%, the price of the bond is 105. Estimate the price of the bond if the yield rate is 3% instead of 4%.
solution_steps: Use the modified duration approximation: \(\%\Delta P \approx -\Delta y \cdot \text{ModD}\).
We are given \(\text{ModD}=6\).
The yield changes from \(4\%\) to \(3\%\), so \(\Delta y=0.03-0.04=-0.01\).
Therefore, \(\%\Delta P \approx -(-0.01)(6)\).
Thus, \(\%\Delta P \approx 0.06\).
So the bond price increases by approximately \(6\%\).
The original price is \(P_{0.04}=105\).
Therefore, the estimated new price is \(P_{0.03}\approx 105(1+0.06)\).
generalization: Modified duration estimates the percentage change in price caused by a change in yield. When the yield decreases, the price increases, so the estimated percentage price change is positive.
answer_text: The estimated price is \(\boxed{111.30}\), so the correct answer is E.

title: Exercise 240
statement: A thirty-year bond with 8% coupons payable quarterly is priced to yield x% convertible quarterly. The modified duration is 8.392 and the Macaulay duration is 8.644. Determine x%.
solution_steps: Use the relationship between modified duration and Macaulay duration: \(\text{ModD}=v\cdot\text{MacD}\).
Here, \(v\) is the discount factor for one interest conversion period.
Since the yield is convertible quarterly, the period is one quarter.
Therefore, \(v=\left(1+\frac{x\%}{4}\right)^{-1}\).
We are given \(\text{ModD}=8.392\) and \(\text{MacD}=8.644\).
Divide both sides by \(8.644\): \(\left(1+\frac{x\%}{4}\right)^{-1}=\frac{8.392}{8.644}\).
This gives \(x\%\approx 0.1201\), so \(x\%\approx 12\%\).
generalization: Modified duration equals Macaulay duration multiplied by the discount factor for one interest conversion period. When the yield is convertible quarterly, use the quarterly discount factor \(v=(1+i^{(4)}/4)^{-1}\).
answer_text: The nominal annual yield rate convertible quarterly is \(\boxed{12\%}\).

title: Exercise 241
statement: A thirty-year bond with 8% coupons payable quarterly is priced to yield 12% convertible quarterly. Calculate the modified duration.
solution_steps: Let the par value be \(1000\).
The quarterly coupon is \(1000(0.08/4)=20\).
The effective yield rate per quarter is \(j=0.12/4=0.03\).
There are \(30(4)=120\) quarterly coupon periods.
To calculate modified duration, use \(\text{ModD}=\frac{v\sum tA_t}{\sum A_t}\), where \(t\) is measured in years and \(v\) is the discount factor per quarter.
The coupon at quarter \(k\) occurs at time \(k/4\) years.
Therefore, \(t\cdot CF_t=(k/4)(20)=5k\).
The maturity payment occurs at time \(30\), so its time-weighted cash flow is \(30(1000)=30000\).
Hence, \(v\sum tA_t=v\left(5(Ia)_{\overline{120}|0.03}+30000v_{0.03}^{120}\right)\).
This gives \(v\sum tA_t=5675.21\).
Therefore, \(\text{ModD}=5675.21/676.27\).
Thus, \(\text{ModD}=8.392\).
generalization: When calculating modified duration with coupon payments more frequent than annual, time must still be measured in years. If coupons are quarterly, the \(k\)-th coupon occurs at time \(k/4\), and the discount factor \(v\) is the per-quarter discount factor.
answer_text: The modified duration is \(\boxed{8.392}\).

title: Exercise 242
statement: Regarding modified duration, which of the following are true? (1) It is the first derivative of price divided by price. (2) It is the percentage increase in price resulting from a 100 basis point increase in yield. (3) For bonds, it is not a function of par value.
choices: The correct answer is not given by A, B, C, or D
solution_steps: Statement 1 is false.
Modified duration is not simply the first derivative of price divided by price. It is the negative of the first derivative of price divided by price: \(\text{ModD}=-\frac{\frac{dP}{di}}{P}\).
The negative sign is needed because bond prices and yields are inversely related.
Statement 2 is false.
Modified duration approximates the percentage change in price using \(\%\Delta P \approx -\Delta i\cdot\text{ModD}\).
A 100 basis point increase in yield means \(\Delta i=0.01\).
Then \(\%\Delta P \approx -0.01\cdot\text{ModD}\), so the price decreases, not increases.
Modified duration can be interpreted as approximately the percentage increase in price resulting from a 100 basis point decrease in yield.
Statement 3 is true.

For bonds, modified duration is not a function of par value. If the par value is multiplied by a constant, then all cash flows and the bond price are multiplied by that same constant, so the factor cancels in the duration calculation.
Thus, only statement 3 is true.
Since this answer is not listed among A, B, C, or D, the correct answer is E.
generalization: Modified duration is defined as \(\text{ModD}=-\frac{\frac{dP}{di}}{P}\). It measures approximate price sensitivity to yield changes and is independent of the scale of the bond's par value.
answer_text: Only statement 3 is true. Therefore, the correct answer is E.

title: Exercise 243
statement: A 10-year bond with quarterly coupons of 8% is priced at par. Calculate the modified duration used to estimate changes in the yield rate convertible quarterly.
solution_steps: Since the bond is priced at par, the coupon rate equals the yield rate, assuming the redemption value equals the face value.
Therefore, the yield rate is \(i^{(4)}=8\%\) convertible quarterly.
The effective quarterly yield rate is \(j=0.08/4=0.02\).
The bond has quarterly coupons for 10 years, so the total number of coupon periods is \(10(4)=40\).
For a par bond with quarterly coupons, the Macaulay duration is \(\text{MacD}=\frac{1}{4}\ddot{a}_{\overline{40}|0.02}\).
Thus, \(\text{MacD}=6.976\).
Modified duration is related to Macaulay duration by \(\text{ModD}=v\cdot\text{MacD}\), where \(v\) is the discount factor for one interest conversion period.
Therefore, \(\text{ModD}=6.976/1.02\).
So, \(\text{ModD}=6.839\).
generalization: For a par bond with coupon payments made \(m\) times per year, first compute Macaulay duration using \(\text{MacD}=\frac{1}{m}\ddot{a}_{\overline{mn}|j}\), then compute modified duration using \(\text{ModD}=v\cdot\text{MacD}\), where \(v=(1+j)^{-1}\).
answer_text: The modified duration is \(\boxed{6.839}\), so the correct answer is C.

title: Exercise 244
statement: Krishna buys an n-year 1000 bond at par. The Macaulay duration is 7.959 years using an annual effective interest rate of 7.2%. Calculate the estimated price of the bond, using the first-order modified approximation, if the interest rate rises to 8.0%.
solution_steps: First find the modified duration.
Since the yield rate is annual effective, the discount factor for one period is \(v=\frac{1}{1+i}\).
Thus, \(\text{ModD}=v\cdot\text{MacD}\).
Equivalently, \(\text{ModD}=\frac{\text{MacD}}{1+i}\).
The interest rate rises from \(7.2\%\) to \(8.0\%\), so \(\Delta i=0.080-0.072=0.008\).
Using the first-order modified duration approximation, \(\%\Delta P\approx -\Delta i\cdot\text{ModD}\).
Since the bond is bought at par, \(P_0=1000\).
Therefore, the estimated new price is \(P_1\approx 1000(1-0.0594)=940.60\).
generalization: To estimate a new bond price using first-order modified duration, first convert Macaulay duration to modified duration using \(\text{ModD}=v\cdot\text{MacD}\). Then use \(\%\Delta P\approx -\Delta i\cdot\text{ModD}\).
answer_text: The estimated price is \(\boxed{940.60}\), so the correct answer is A.

title: Exercise 245
statement: A portfolio contains three bonds, all of which have par value 1000. The first has a two-year term and no coupons, the second is a three-year bond with no coupons, and the third is a two-year 6% bond with semiannual coupons. If each bond is purchased to yield 5% convertible semiannually, find the modified duration of the portfolio.
solution_steps: The nominal annual yield rate is \(5\%\) convertible semiannually, so the effective yield rate per half-year is \(j=0.05/2=0.025\).
Bond I is a two-year zero-coupon bond, so \(P_I=1000v^4=1000(1.025)^{-4}=905.95\).
Since Bond I is a zero-coupon bond with maturity at time 2, \(\text{MacD}_I=2\).
Bond II is a three-year zero-coupon bond, so \(P_{II}=1000v^6=1000(1.025)^{-6}=862.30\).
Since Bond II is a zero-coupon bond with maturity at time 3, \(\text{MacD}_{II}=3\).
Bond III is a two-year 6% bond with semiannual coupons. The semiannual coupon is \(1000(0.06/2)=30\).
The price of Bond III is \(P_{III}=30v+30v^2+30v^3+1030v^4=1018.81\).
The Macaulay duration of Bond III is \(\text{MacD}_{III}=\frac{0.5(30)v+1(30)v^2+1.5(30)v^3+2(1030)v^4}{1018.81}\).
Thus, \(\text{MacD}_{III}=1.9152\).
The portfolio Macaulay duration is the price-weighted average: \(\text{MacD}_P=\frac{P_I\text{MacD}_I+P_{II}\text{MacD}_{II}+P_{III}\text{MacD}_{III}}{P_I+P_{II}+P_{III}}\).
Substitute the values: \(\text{MacD}_P=\frac{2P_I+3P_{II}+1.9152P_{III}}{P_I+P_{II}+P_{III}}\).
This gives \(\text{MacD}_P=2.2784\).
Modified duration is \(\text{ModD}_P=v\cdot\text{MacD}_P\).
generalization: The duration of a portfolio is the price-weighted average of the durations of its components. After finding the portfolio Macaulay duration, multiply by the per-period discount factor to obtain modified duration when the yield is convertible periodically.
answer_text: The modified duration of the portfolio is \(\boxed{2.2228}\).

title: Exercise 246
statement: A 5000 loan is being repaid over a two-year period with level payments at the end of each quarter. The interest rate is 8% convertible quarterly. Find the jump in the duration of the loan following the 5th payment.
solution_steps: The nominal annual interest rate is \(8\%\) convertible quarterly, so the effective quarterly interest rate is \(j=0.08/4=0.02\).
The loan is repaid over 2 years with quarterly payments, so there are \(2(4)=8\) payments.
We want the jump in duration following the 5th payment.
Just before the 5th payment, there are four remaining payments: one immediately, and then three more at times \(0.25\), \(0.50\), and \(0.75\).
Let the level payment be \(R\). The value of \(R\) is not needed because it cancels in the duration formula.
Just before the 5th payment, \(\text{MacD(before)}=\frac{0\cdot R+0.25Rv+0.50Rv^2+0.75Rv^3}{R(1+v+v^2+v^3)}\).
Canceling \(R\), \(\text{MacD(before)}=\frac{0.25v+0.50v^2+0.75v^3}{1+v+v^2+v^3}\).
Substituting \(v=1/1.02\), we get \(\text{MacD(before)}=0.369\).
Immediately after the 5th payment, the payment at time 0 has been made. The remaining cash flows occur at times \(0.25\), \(0.50\), and \(0.75\).
Thus, \(\text{MacD(after)}=\frac{0.25Rv+0.50Rv^2+0.75Rv^3}{R(v+v^2+v^3)}\).
Canceling \(R\), \(\text{MacD(after)}=\frac{0.25v+0.50v^2+0.75v^3}{v+v^2+v^3}\).
Substituting \(v=1/1.02\), we get \(\text{MacD(after)}=0.497\).
Therefore, the jump in duration is \(0.497-0.369=0.128\).
generalization: Immediately after a cash flow is paid, duration can jump upward because the earliest remaining cash flow has been removed. For level payments, the payment amount cancels out of the duration calculation.
answer_text: The jump in duration is \(\boxed{0.128}\).

title: Exercise 247
statement: Sam buys an eight-year, 5000 par bond with an annual coupon rate of 5%, paid annually. The bond sells for 5000. Let d_1 be the Macaulay duration just before the first coupon is paid. Let d_2 be the Macaulay duration just after the first coupon is paid. Calculate d_1/d_2.
solution_steps: Since the bond is priced at par, the yield rate equals the coupon rate: \(i=0.05\).
The annual coupon is \(5000(0.05)=250\).
Just before the first coupon is paid, the coupon due immediately has time \(0\), and the remaining cash flows occur at times \(1,2,\ldots,7\).
Just after the first coupon is paid, the immediate coupon is no longer part of the remaining cash flows.
The numerators of \(d_1\) and \(d_2\) are the same because the immediate coupon in \(d_1\) is multiplied by \(0\).
Since the bond is priced at par, immediately after the first coupon is paid, the remaining seven-year bond has value \(250a_{\overline{7}|0.05}+5000v^7=5000\).
Rounded to two decimal places, \(d_1/d_2=0.95\).
generalization: Immediately before a coupon payment, that coupon is included as an immediate cash flow, but it contributes zero to the numerator of Macaulay duration because its time is zero. Immediately after the coupon is paid, it is removed from the remaining cash flows. This changes the denominator and causes duration to jump.
answer_text: The ratio is \(\boxed{0.95}\), so the correct answer is C.

title: Exercise 248
statement: A five-year bond with annual coupons of 100 and a redemption amount of 1500 is priced at 1608.24 to yield 5%. The duration of this bond is 4.435. Estimate the percent change in price that would occur if the yield rose to 5.5%.
solution_steps: Method 1: Use the Macaulay duration approximation for the percent change in price: \(\%\Delta P \approx \left(\frac{1+i_0}{1+i_1}\right)^{\text{MacD}}-1\).
Substitute the values: \(\%\Delta P \approx \left(\frac{1.05}{1.055}\right)^{4.435}-1\).
This gives \(\%\Delta P \approx -0.02085\).
Therefore, the bond price is estimated to decrease by approximately \(2.085\%\).
Method 2: Estimate the new price first using \(P_{i_1}\approx P_{i_0}\left(\frac{1+i_0}{1+i_1}\right)^{\text{MacD}}\).
Substitute the values: \(P_{i_1}\approx 1608.24\left(\frac{1.05}{1.055}\right)^{4.435}\).
This gives \(P_{i_1}\approx 1574.71\).
Now compute the percent change manually: \(\%\Delta P=\frac{P_{i_1}-P_{i_0}}{P_{i_0}}\).
Both methods give the same estimated percent change.
generalization: The Macaulay duration approximation can be used either to estimate the percent change directly or to estimate the new price first. The price formula is \(P_{i_1}\approx P_{i_0}\left(\frac{1+i_0}{1+i_1}\right)^{\text{MacD}}\), and then the percent change can be computed manually.
answer_text: The estimated percent change in price is \(\boxed{-0.02085}\), which means the bond price decreases by approximately \(2.085\%\).

title: Exercise 249
statement: A four-year bond with semiannual coupons is priced at 1179.25 to yield 5% convertible semiannually. Suppose the bond's price rises to 1199.30, implying a new nominal yield of 4.5% convertible semiannually. Estimate the Macaulay duration of the bond prior to the interest rate change.
solution_steps: Use the Macaulay duration approximation: \(P_{i_1}\approx P_{i_0}\left(\frac{1+i_0}{1+i_1}\right)^{\text{MacD}}\), where \(i_0\) and \(i_1\) are annual effective yield rates.
The original yield is \(5\%\) convertible semiannually, so the semiannual rate is \(0.05/2=0.025\).
Thus the original annual accumulation factor is \((1.025)^2\).
The new yield is \(4.5\%\) convertible semiannually, so the semiannual rate is \(0.045/2=0.0225\).
Thus the new annual accumulation factor is \((1.0225)^2\).
Substitute into the approximation: \(1199.30\approx 1179.25\left(\frac{1.025^2}{1.0225^2}\right)^{\text{MacD}}\).
Divide both sides by \(1179.25\): \(\frac{1199.30}{1179.25}\approx \left(\frac{1.025^2}{1.0225^2}\right)^{\text{MacD}}\).
Take natural logarithms: \(\ln\left(\frac{1199.30}{1179.25}\right)\approx \text{MacD}\cdot \ln\left(\frac{1.025^2}{1.0225^2}\right)\).
Solve for Macaulay duration: \(\text{MacD}\approx \frac{\ln\left(\frac{1199.30}{1179.25}\right)}{\ln\left(\frac{1.025^2}{1.0225^2}\right)}\).
Therefore, \(\text{MacD}\approx 3.452\).
generalization: When using the Macaulay duration approximation with nominal rates convertible more than once per year, convert the yields into annual accumulation factors before applying the formula. Then solve for the unknown duration using logarithms.
answer_text: The estimated Macaulay duration is \(\boxed{3.452}\).

title: Exercise 250
statement: A 3-year bond with semiannual coupons has a duration of 2.7342 years. The current yield on the bond is 6% convertible semiannually. Use a Macaulay duration approximation to estimate the new interest rate, convertible semiannually, that would give a price increase of 5%.
solution_steps: Use the Macaulay duration approximation: \(P_{i_1}\approx P_{i_0}\left(\frac{1+i_0}{1+i_1}\right)^{\text{MacD}}\).
This gives the percent change approximation: \(\%\Delta P\approx \left(\frac{1+i_0}{1+i_1}\right)^{\text{MacD}}-1\).
The current yield is \(6\%\) convertible semiannually, so the semiannual yield rate is \(0.06/2=0.03\).
Therefore, the annual effective accumulation factor is \((1.03)^2=1.0609\).
The price increase is \(5\%\), so \(\%\Delta P=0.05\).
Using \(\text{MacD}=2.7342\), we have \(0.05\approx \left(\frac{1.0609}{1+i_1}\right)^{2.7342}-1\).
Therefore, \(1.05\approx \left(\frac{1.0609}{1+i_1}\right)^{2.7342}\).
Taking both sides to the power \(1/2.7342\), \(1.05^{1/2.7342}\approx \frac{1.0609}{1+i_1}\).
This is the new interest rate expressed as an annual effective rate.
Convert this annual effective rate to a nominal annual rate convertible semiannually using \(1+i_1=\left(1+\frac{i^{(2)}}{2}\right)^2\).
So the new yield rate convertible semiannually is \(4.17\%\).
generalization: When using the Macaulay duration approximation with nominal rates convertible semiannually, convert the nominal rate to an annual effective accumulation factor before applying the approximation. After solving for the new annual effective rate, convert it back to the requested nominal convertible rate.
answer_text: The new yield rate convertible semiannually is \(\boxed{4.17\%}\), so the correct answer is A.

title: Exercise 251
statement: Bob owns a 10-year bond with annual coupons, which has a present value of P_0. Suppose the yield rate changes, and Bob estimates the new price P_1 using duration, giving an estimated new value of P_1^{MacD}, and modified duration, giving an estimated new value of P_1^{ModD}. Which of the following relationships can possibly be true?
choices: More than one of the above could be true
solution_steps: The modified duration approximation always underestimates the new price, so \(P_1^{\text{ModD}}<P_1\).
When all future cash flows are positive, the Macaulay duration approximation is closer to the actual new price than the modified duration approximation.
Since Bob owns a bond with annual coupons, all future cash flows are positive.
Therefore, the Macaulay duration approximation must be closer to \(P_1\) than the modified duration approximation.
Putting these facts together, two arrangements are possible: \(P_1^{\text{ModD}}<P_1^{\text{MacD}}<P_1\), or \(P_1^{\text{ModD}}<P_1<P_1^{\text{MacD}}\).
The second arrangement is not among the answer choices.
The only listed relationship that can possibly be true is \(P_1^{\text{ModD}}<P_1^{\text{MacD}}<P_1\).
generalization: For bonds with positive future cash flows, the Macaulay duration approximation is generally closer to the actual new price than the modified duration approximation. Modified duration underestimates the new price, so the Macaulay estimate may lie either between the modified duration estimate and the true price, or on the other side of the true price.
answer_text: The correct answer is \(\boxed{\text{B}}\).

title: Exercise 252
statement: A three-year bond with 8% annual coupons has a redemption value of 105% of the par value and is priced to yield an annual effective rate of 6%. Calculate the convexity.
solution_steps: The par value does not matter because it cancels in the convexity calculation. Let the par value be \(100\).
The annual coupon is \(100(0.08)=8\).
The redemption value is \(100(1.05)=105\).
Thus, the cash flows are \(8\) at time 1, \(8\) at time 2, and \(8+105=113\) at time 3.
The yield rate is annual effective, so \(i=0.06\).
Since the yield is annual effective, use \(m=1\). Therefore, the convexity formula is \(\text{Convexity}=\frac{\sum_t t(t+1)A_t v^2}{\sum_t A_t}\).
The denominator is the bond price: \(\sum_t A_t=8(1.06)^{-1}+8(1.06)^{-2}+113(1.06)^{-3}\).
The numerator is \(\sum_t t(t+1)A_t v^2\).
Therefore, \(\sum_t t(t+1)A_t v^2=1064.75\).
Hence, \(\text{Convexity}=\frac{1064.75}{109.54}=9.72\).
generalization: For annual effective yield rates, convexity is calculated using \(\text{Convexity}=\frac{\sum_t t(t+1)A_t v^2}{\sum_t A_t}\), where \(A_t\) is the present value of the cash flow at time \(t\). The par value cancels because both the numerator and denominator scale proportionally with the cash flows.
answer_text: The convexity is \(\boxed{9.72}\).

title: Exercise 253
statement: Which of the following are true? (1) The duration of the portfolio is the weighted average of the duration of each asset in the portfolio, weighted by the price of each asset. (2) Using the modified duration to estimate the change in price for an asset always underestimates the price. (3) The convexity of a fixed cash flow asset is always negative.
choices: The correct answer is not given by A, B, C, or D
solution_steps: Statement 1 is true.
The duration of a portfolio is the price-weighted average of the durations of the assets in the portfolio: \(\text{Duration}_P=\frac{\sum_k P_k\text{Duration}_k}{\sum_k P_k}\).
Therefore, the weights are based on the price of each asset.
Statement 2 is true.
The modified duration approximation uses a linear approximation to the price-yield curve: \(\%\Delta P \approx -\Delta i\cdot \text{ModD}\).
Because the price-yield curve is convex, this linear approximation underestimates the actual new price.
Statement 3 is false.
For a fixed cash flow asset with positive cash flows, convexity is positive.
The convexity formula is \(\text{Convexity}=\frac{\sum_t t\left(t+\frac{1}{m}\right)A_t v^2}{\sum_t A_t}\).
If all cash flows are positive, then all present values \(A_t\) are positive. Therefore, the convexity is positive, not negative.
Thus, only statements 1 and 2 are true.
generalization: Portfolio duration is calculated as a price-weighted average. Modified duration gives a linear approximation to the price-yield curve, while convexity measures the curvature of that relationship. For fixed positive cash flows, convexity is positive.
answer_text: Only statements 1 and 2 are true. Therefore, the correct answer is \(\boxed{\text{A}}\).

title: Exercise 254
statement: Calculate the convexity of a 4-year annuity-immediate with payments of 100, 100, 200, and 300. The annual effective yield rate is 6%.
solution_steps: The payments are made at the end of each year, so the cash flows are \(100\), \(100\), \(200\), and \(300\) at times \(1\), \(2\), \(3\), and \(4\).
The annual effective yield rate is \(y=0.06\).
The price is the present value of the cash flows: \(P=100v+100v^2+200v^3+300v^4\), where \(v=1/1.06\).
Convexity is defined by \(\text{Convexity}=P''/P\).
Therefore, \(\text{Convexity}=6666.36/588.89\).
Thus, \(\text{Convexity}\approx 11.320\).
generalization: For annual effective yield rates, convexity may be calculated directly as \(P''/P\), where \(P\) is the present value of the cash flows as a function of the yield rate.
answer_text: The convexity is \(\boxed{11.320}\), so the correct answer is A.

title: Exercise 255
statement: A three-year bond with 8% annual coupons has a redemption value of 105% of the par value and is priced to yield an annual effective rate of 6%. Calculate the Macaulay convexity.
solution_steps: The par value does not matter because it cancels in the Macaulay convexity calculation. Let the par value be \(100\).
The annual coupon is \(100(0.08)=8\).
The redemption value is \(100(1.05)=105\).
Thus, the cash flows are \(8\) at time 1, \(8\) at time 2, and \(8+105=113\) at time 3.
The annual effective yield rate is \(i=0.06\), so \(v=1/1.06\).
Macaulay convexity is \(\text{MacC}=\frac{\sum_t t^2A_t}{\sum_t A_t}\), where \(A_t\) is the present value of the cash flow at time \(t\).
The denominator is \(\sum_t A_t=8v+8v^2+113v^3\).
generalization: Macaulay convexity is the present-value-weighted average of \(t^2\). It is calculated using \(\text{MacC}=\frac{\sum_t t^2A_t}{\sum_t A_t}\), where \(t\) is measured in years.
answer_text: The Macaulay convexity is \(\boxed{8.124}\).

title: Exercise 256
statement: We construct a portfolio of zero-coupon bonds. The price of a 100 par value zero-coupon bond is 94.12 for a 1-year term, 88.17 for a 2-year term, and 82.55 for a 3-year term. The portfolio has 1000 par value of 1-year bonds, 2500 par value of 2-year bonds, and 2000 par value of 3-year bonds. Find the Macaulay convexity of the portfolio.
solution_steps: Use the price-weighted average of the Macaulay convexities.
Since the quoted prices are for 100 par value zero-coupon bonds, the prices of the portfolio positions are \(P_I=10(94.12)=941.20\), \(P_{II}=25(88.17)=2204.25\), and \(P_{III}=20(82.55)=1651.00\).
For a zero-coupon bond, there is only one cash flow. Therefore, the Macaulay convexity is \(\text{MacC}=t^2\).
Thus, \(\text{MacC}(I)=1^2=1\), \(\text{MacC}(II)=2^2=4\), and \(\text{MacC}(III)=3^2=9\).
The portfolio Macaulay convexity is the price-weighted average: \(\text{MacC}_P=\frac{P_I\text{MacC}(I)+P_{II}\text{MacC}(II)+P_{III}\text{MacC}(III)}{P_I+P_{II}+P_{III}}\).
Substitute the values: \(\text{MacC}_P=\frac{1P_I+4P_{II}+9P_{III}}{P_I+P_{II}+P_{III}}\).
generalization: For a portfolio, Macaulay convexity is the price-weighted average of the Macaulay convexities of the individual assets. For a zero-coupon bond with a single cash flow at time \(t\), the Macaulay convexity is \(t^2\).
answer_text: The Macaulay convexity of the portfolio is \(\boxed{5.132}\).

title: Exercise 257
statement: You are given the following information about a bond: (i) The term-to-maturity is two years. (ii) The bond has a 9% annual coupon rate, paid semiannually. (iii) The annual yield to maturity is 8% convertible semiannually. (iv) The par value is $100. Calculate the Macaulay convexity of the bond.
solution_steps: The annual coupon rate is 9%, paid semiannually. Therefore each coupon is \(\frac{0.09}{2}(100)=4.5\).
The annual yield rate is 8% convertible semiannually, so the semiannual yield rate is \(j=0.08/2=0.04\).
The bond matures in two years, so the cash flows are \(4.5,4.5,4.5,104.5\) at times \(0.5,1,1.5,2\), respectively.
Numerically, \(P=101.81495\).
For Macaulay convexity, use \(\operatorname{MacC}=\frac{\sum_t t^2 A_t}{\sum_t A_t}\), where \(A_t\) is the present value of the cash flow paid at time \(t\).
Since \(3.64928<4\), the correct answer is A.
generalization: For Macaulay convexity, the times must be expressed in years when the question asks for Macaulay convexity in annual time units. Each cash flow is weighted by the square of its payment time and by its present value.
answer_text: A. Less than 4

title: Exercise 258
statement: True or False: Redington immunization protects a surplus against any changes in the interest rate.
solution_steps: The statement is false.
Redington immunization protects a surplus only against small changes in the interest rate.
The Redington immunization conditions are \(P_A=P_L\), \(\operatorname{MacD}_A=\operatorname{MacD}_L\), and \(\operatorname{MacC}_A>\operatorname{MacC}_L\).
These conditions imply that the surplus \(S(i)=P_A(i)-P_L(i)\) has a local minimum at the current interest rate.
Therefore, for small changes in the interest rate, the surplus should not decrease.
However, if the change in interest rates is large, the surplus may still decline.
Thus, the correct answer is False.
generalization: Redington immunization is a local immunization strategy. It protects against small changes in the interest rate, but it does not guarantee protection against all possible interest rate changes.
answer_text: B. False

title: Exercise 259
statement: Infinite Life buys a 5-year zero-coupon bond that will mature for $1,000 to immunize two liability cash flows of X and Y. X occurs in 3 years and Y occurs in 10 years. Find X and Y such that Conditions 1 and 2 of Redington immunization are satisfied if the effective annual rate of interest is 6%.
solution_steps: The asset is a 5-year zero-coupon bond that matures for \(1000\), so its present value is \(P_A=1000v^5\).
The liabilities are \(X\) at time 3 and \(Y\) at time 10, so their present value is \(P_L=Xv^3+Yv^{10}\).
Condition 1 of Redington immunization requires \(P_A=P_L\). Therefore, \(1000v^5=Xv^3+Yv^{10}\).
Condition 2 requires \(\operatorname{MacD}_A=\operatorname{MacD}_L\). Since the asset is a 5-year zero-coupon bond, \(\operatorname{MacD}_A=5\).
The Macaulay duration of the liabilities is \(\operatorname{MacD}_L=\frac{3Xv^3+10Yv^{10}}{Xv^3+Yv^{10}}\).
Set this equal to 5: \(5=\frac{3Xv^3+10Yv^{10}}{Xv^3+Yv^{10}}\).
Multiplying through gives \(5(Xv^3+Yv^{10})=3Xv^3+10Yv^{10}\).
Simplifying gives \(2Xv^3=5Yv^{10}\), so \(Xv^3=\frac{5}{2}Yv^{10}\).
Substitute this into Condition 1: \(1000v^5=\frac{5}{2}Yv^{10}+Yv^{10}\).
Using \(2Xv^3=5Yv^{10}\), we get \(X=\frac{5}{2}Yv^7\).
generalization: To satisfy the first two Redington immunization conditions, match the present value of the assets and liabilities, and match their Macaulay durations. For zero-coupon cash flows, the Macaulay duration is simply the payment time.

title: Exercise 260
statement: Infinite Life buys a 5-year zero-coupon bond that will mature for $1,000 to immunize two liability cash flows of X and Y. The liability X occurs in 3 years and the liability Y occurs in 10 years. Find X and Y such that Conditions 1 and 2 of Redington immunization are satisfied if the effective annual rate of interest is 6%. If Infinite Life buys the bond and guarantees the payments of X and Y, has it achieved Redington immunization?
correct: X = 635.71, Y = 382.35, and Redington immunization has not been achieved.
solution_steps: The asset is a 5-year zero-coupon bond that matures for \(1000\), so \(P_A=1000v^5\).
The liabilities are \(X\) at time 3 and \(Y\) at time 10, so \(P_L=Xv^3+Yv^{10}\).
Condition 1 requires \(P_A=P_L\), so \(1000v^5=Xv^3+Yv^{10}\).
Condition 2 requires \(\operatorname{MacD}_A=\operatorname{MacD}_L\). Since the asset is a 5-year zero-coupon bond, \(\operatorname{MacD}_A=5\).
The Macaulay duration of the liabilities is \(\operatorname{MacD}_L=\frac{3Xv^3+10Yv^{10}}{Xv^3+Yv^{10}}\).
Set the liability duration equal to 5: \(5=\frac{3Xv^3+10Yv^{10}}{Xv^3+Yv^{10}}\).
Multiplying through gives \(5(Xv^3+Yv^{10})=3Xv^3+10Yv^{10}\).
Simplifying gives \(2Xv^3=5Yv^{10}\), so \(Xv^3=\frac{5}{2}Yv^{10}\).
Substitute this into Condition 1: \(1000v^5=\frac{5}{2}Yv^{10}+Yv^{10}=\frac{7}{2}Yv^{10}\).
Now check Condition 3. Since the asset is a 5-year zero-coupon bond, \(\operatorname{MacC}_A=5^2=25\).
The Macaulay convexity of the liabilities is \(\operatorname{MacC}_L=\frac{3^2Xv^3+10^2Yv^{10}}{Xv^3+Yv^{10}}\).
Substituting \(X=635.71\), \(Y=382.35\), and \(v=(1.06)^{-1}\), we obtain \(\operatorname{MacC}_L=35\).
Condition 3 requires \(\operatorname{MacC}_A>\operatorname{MacC}_L\), but \(25\not>35\).
Therefore, Redington immunization has not been achieved.
generalization: Matching present value and Macaulay duration satisfies only the first two Redington immunization conditions. Full Redington immunization also requires the Macaulay convexity of the assets to exceed the Macaulay convexity of the liabilities.
answer_text: X = 635.71, Y = 382.35, and Redington immunization has not been achieved because Condition 3 fails.

title: Exercise 261
statement: Regarding Redington immunization, which of the following is not true?
choices: Assumes only parallel shifts in the yield curve.
Protects the surplus from small shifts in the yield curve.
The third condition is: P_A'' = P_L''.
All of the above are true.
solution_steps: Redington immunization is a local immunization strategy.
It assumes a flat yield curve and protects the surplus against small parallel shifts in the yield curve.
Let \(S(i)=P_A(i)-P_L(i)\), where \(P_A(i)\) is the present value of the assets and \(P_L(i)\) is the present value of the liabilities.
The Redington immunization conditions are \(P_A=P_L\), \(P_A'=P_L'\), and \(P_A''>P_L''\).
Equivalently, the surplus satisfies \(S(i)=0\), \(S'(i)=0\), and \(S''(i)>0\).
The third condition is not \(P_A''=P_L''\). It is \(P_A''>P_L''\).
Therefore, the statement in choice D is not true.
generalization: The third Redington condition requires the asset present value function to have greater second derivative than the liability present value function at the current yield. This creates a local minimum in the surplus function.
answer_text: D. The third condition is not P_A'' = P_L''. It is P_A'' > P_L''.

title: Exercise 262
statement: A company has liabilities of 573 due at the end of year 2 and 701 due at the end of year 5. A portfolio comprises two zero-coupon bonds, Bond A and Bond B. Determine which portfolio produces a Redington immunization of the liabilities using an annual effective interest rate of 7.0%.
choices: Bond A: 1-year, current price 500; Bond B: 6-years, current price 500
Bond A: 1-year, current price 572; Bond B: 6-years, current price 428
Bond A: 3-years, current price 182; Bond B: 4-years, current price 1092
Bond A: 3-years, current price 637; Bond B: 4-years, current price 637
Bond A: 3.5-years, current price 1000; Bond B: not used
solution_steps: The present value of the liabilities is \(P_L=573v^2+701v^5\).
Condition 1 of Redington immunization requires \(P_A=P_L\). Since the current price of each bond is its present value, the asset portfolio must have total current price approximately 1000.
Choices C and D have total current price \(182+1092=1274\) and \(637+637=1274\), so they are eliminated.
The Macaulay duration of the liabilities is \(\operatorname{MacD}_L=\frac{2(573v^2)+5(701v^5)}{573v^2+701v^5}\approx 3.5\).
For a zero-coupon bond, the Macaulay duration is the maturity of the bond. For a portfolio, Macaulay duration is the price-weighted average of the component durations.
For choice A, \(\operatorname{MacD}_A=\frac{500}{1000}(1)+\frac{500}{1000}(6)=3.5\), so choice A satisfies Condition 2.
For choice B, \(\operatorname{MacD}_A=\frac{572}{1000}(1)+\frac{428}{1000}(6)=3.14\), so choice B does not satisfy Condition 2.
Choice E has a single 3.5-year zero-coupon bond, so \(\operatorname{MacD}_A=3.5\). Thus, after Conditions 1 and 2, choices A and E remain.
Condition 3 requires \(\operatorname{MacC}_A>\operatorname{MacC}_L\).
The Macaulay convexity of the liabilities is \(\operatorname{MacC}_L=\frac{2^2(573v^2)+5^2(701v^5)}{573v^2+701v^5}\approx 14.5\).
For a zero-coupon bond, the Macaulay convexity is the square of its maturity. For a portfolio, Macaulay convexity is the price-weighted average of the component convexities.
For choice A, \(\operatorname{MacC}_A=\frac{500}{1000}(1^2)+\frac{500}{1000}(6^2)=18.5\). Since \(18.5>14.5\), choice A satisfies Condition 3.
For choice E, \(\operatorname{MacC}_A=3.5^2=12.25\). Since \(12.25<14.5\), choice E does not satisfy Condition 3.
Therefore, the portfolio that produces a Redington immunization is choice A.
generalization: For Redington immunization, the asset portfolio must match the present value of the liabilities, match the Macaulay duration of the liabilities, and have greater Macaulay convexity than the liabilities. For zero-coupon bonds, Macaulay duration is the maturity and Macaulay convexity is the maturity squared.
answer_text: A. Bond A: 1-year, current price 500; Bond B: 6-years, current price 500

title: Exercise 263
statement: Which of the following are ways to state the three conditions of Redington immunization? 1. A. PV(Assets) = PV(Liabilities), B. Duration(Assets) = Duration(Liabilities), C. Convexity(Assets) > Convexity(Liabilities). 2. A. Price(Assets) = Price(Liabilities), B. Price'(Assets) = Price'(Liabilities), C. Price''(Assets) > Price''(Liabilities). 3. A. Surplus(y) = 0, B. Surplus'(y) = 0, C. Surplus''(y) > 0.
choices: The correct answer is not given by A, B, C, or D
solution_steps: The classic Redington immunization conditions are \(PV(\text{Assets})=PV(\text{Liabilities})\), \(\operatorname{Duration}(\text{Assets})=\operatorname{Duration}(\text{Liabilities})\), and \(\operatorname{Convexity}(\text{Assets})>\operatorname{Convexity}(\text{Liabilities})\). Therefore, statement 1 is valid.
Let \(P_A(y)\) be the price of the assets as a function of the yield rate \(y\), and let \(P_L(y)\) be the price of the liabilities.
The conditions can also be written as \(P_A(y)=P_L(y)\), \(P_A'(y)=P_L'(y)\), and \(P_A''(y)>P_L''(y)\).
The first derivative condition corresponds to matching first-order sensitivity to changes in the yield rate, which is equivalent to matching duration.
The second derivative condition corresponds to the convexity condition. Therefore, statement 2 is valid.
Define the surplus function by \(S(y)=P_A(y)-P_L(y)\).
Redington immunization requires the surplus to have a local minimum at the current yield rate, so \(S(y)=0\), \(S'(y)=0\), and \(S''(y)>0\). Therefore, statement 3 is valid.
Since statements 1, 2, and 3 are all valid ways to state the Redington immunization conditions, the correct answer is D.
generalization: Redington immunization can be stated in three equivalent ways: by matching present values, durations, and convexities; by comparing derivatives of asset and liability price functions; or by requiring the surplus function to have a local minimum at the current yield rate.
answer_text: D. 1, 2 and 3

title: Exercise 264
statement: A life insurance company has written a policy that pays $100,000 one year after the death of the policyholder. What makes this liability difficult to immunize?
correct: The timing of the liability is uncertain.
solution_steps: The liability is difficult to immunize because the timing of the liability is uncertain.
The insurance company knows the amount that will be paid: \(100,000\).
However, the company does not know when the policyholder will die. Since the payment is made one year after death, the payment date is also uncertain.
This creates a problem for exact matching. In a dedication strategy, asset cash flows are chosen so that they exactly match liability cash flows at the same times.
Because the payment date is unknown, the company does not know when the asset cash flow should arrive.

The uncertain timing also makes it difficult to satisfy the duration condition in Redington or full immunization. The duration of the liability depends on the timing of the payment, but that timing is unknown.
There is also a practical issue. If the policyholder lives for many decades, the liability may not be due for a very long time.
It may be difficult to find assets with sufficiently long maturities to match this liability.
Thus, the liability is difficult to immunize because its timing is uncertain, exact matching is difficult, its duration is uncertain, and long-maturity assets may not be available.
generalization: Liabilities with uncertain timing are difficult to immunize because both exact matching and duration matching require knowledge of when the liability cash flows will occur.
answer_text: The timing of the liability is uncertain. This makes exact matching and duration matching difficult, and the required asset maturities may not be available.

title: Exercise 265
statement: A company must pay L one year from now and 2L two years from now. The company buys two bonds to match the liability cash flows. The first bond is a $1000 one-year bond. The second bond is a $2500 two-year bond. Both bonds pay annual coupons at a rate r. Calculate L.
solution_steps: The company wants to exactly match the liability cash flows.
The liabilities are \(L\) at time 1 and \(2L\) at time 2.
The one-year bond has face value \(1000\), so it pays \(1000+1000r\) at time 1.
The two-year bond has face value \(2500\), so it pays a coupon \(2500r\) at time 1 and principal plus coupon \(2500+2500r\) at time 2.
At time 2, exact matching gives \(2L=2500+2500r\).
At time 1, exact matching gives \(L=(1000+1000r)+2500r\).
Substitute this into the time 2 equation: \(2(1000+3500r)=2500+2500r\).
Expanding gives \(2000+7000r=2500+2500r\).
Solving gives \(4500r=500\), so \(r=\frac{1}{9}\).
generalization: In a dedication problem, asset cash flows are matched exactly to liability cash flows at each payment time. This produces a system of equations, one equation for each liability payment date.

title: Exercise 266
statement: Which of the following are true? 1. Full immunization protects the surplus for interest rate changes of any size. 2. Full immunization does not assume a flat yield curve like Redington immunization does. 3. If full immunization is achieved, then Redington immunization is achieved.
choices: The correct answer is not given by A, B, C, or D
solution_steps: Statement 1 is true. Full immunization is designed to protect the surplus against interest rate changes of any size, not only small changes.
This is stronger than Redington immunization, which protects the surplus only locally against small changes in the interest rate.
However, this statement is understood under the usual immunization framework, where interest rate changes are modeled as changes in a single yield rate or as parallel shifts.
Statement 2 is false. Full immunization still relies on a simplified interest rate framework, such as a flat yield curve or parallel shifts in the yield curve.
The method that avoids the need for a flat yield curve or parallel shifts is dedication, also called exact cash flow matching.
Under dedication, asset cash flows exactly match liability cash flows at each time, so \(CF_{A,t}=CF_{L,t}\) for every relevant time \(t\).
Statement 3 is true. Full immunization is stronger than Redington immunization.
Therefore, if full immunization is achieved, then Redington immunization is also achieved.
Thus, statements 1 and 3 are true, while statement 2 is false.
generalization: Redington immunization gives local protection against small interest rate changes. Full immunization gives stronger protection against changes of any size under the standard flat-yield or parallel-shift framework. Dedication is exact cash flow matching and does not require a flat yield curve or parallel shifts.
answer_text: B. 1 and 3 only

title: Exercise 267
statement: Which of the following is not a problem with dedication?
choices: Asset cash flows are not always easy to predict.
Assets may not be available to exactly match all the liabilities.
Liability cash flows are often just estimates.
A higher yield might be available.
solution_steps: Dedication is immunization by exact cash flow matching.
The goal is to choose asset cash flows so that they exactly match the liability cash flows at the same times.
Mathematically, dedication aims to satisfy \(CF_{A,t}=CF_{L,t}\) for each relevant time \(t\).
If the asset and liability cash flows are known and exactly matched, then the strategy does not normally require continuous rebalancing.
Therefore, requiring rebalancing is not a typical problem with dedication.
The other choices describe real problems with dedication.
Asset cash flows may not always be easy to predict, especially if bonds are callable or loans can be prepaid.
Assets may not be available with the exact maturities and amounts needed to match all liabilities.
Liability cash flows may be uncertain or based on estimates, especially in insurance contexts.
Dedication may also force the investor to choose lower-yielding assets in order to match liabilities exactly.
Thus, the correct answer is A.
generalization: Dedication reduces interest rate risk by exactly matching asset cash flows to liability cash flows. Its main weaknesses are uncertainty in asset or liability cash flows, lack of suitable matching assets, and possible opportunity cost from giving up higher-yielding investments.
answer_text: A. Requires rebalancing.

title: Exercise 268
statement: A company's expected liabilities are as follows, assuming end-of-year payments: 100 at year 1, 200 at year 2, and 300 at year 3. The company can buy bonds in any face amount. The available bonds have maturities of 1 year, 2 years, and 3 years, with coupon rates of 5%, 6%, and 0%, respectively. Assume a flat yield curve with a yield-to-maturity of 6%. The company uses cash flow matching to determine how much of each bond to buy. Calculate how much the company spends on 1-year bonds.
solution_steps: Let \(F_1\), \(F_2\), and \(F_3\) denote the face amounts of the 1-year, 2-year, and 3-year bonds, respectively.
Since the company uses cash flow matching, work backward from the final liability.
At time 3, the liability is \(300\). The 3-year bond is a zero-coupon bond, so it pays only its face amount at maturity. Therefore, \(F_3=300\).
At time 2, the liability is \(200\). The 2-year bond has a coupon rate of 6%, so it pays \(1.06F_2\) at time 2.
At time 1, the liability is \(100\). The 1-year bond pays \(1.05F_1\), and the 2-year bond pays a coupon of \(0.06F_2\).
Therefore, \(F_1=\frac{88.68}{1.05}=84.46\). This is the face amount of the 1-year bond.
The question asks how much the company spends on 1-year bonds, which is the current price of the 1-year bond.
Since the yield-to-maturity is 6%, the price is \(\frac{1.05F_1}{1.06}\).
generalization: In dedication problems, work backward from the final liability. First determine the face amount needed for the longest maturity bond, then subtract its earlier coupons from earlier liabilities. The amount spent on a bond is its current price, not necessarily its face amount.

title: Exercise 269
statement: Investment X for $100,000 is invested at a nominal rate of interest, j, convertible semiannually. After four years, it accumulates to $214,358.88. Investment Y for $100,000 is invested at a nominal rate of discount, k, convertible quarterly. After two years, it accumulates to $232,305.73. Investment Z for $100,000 is invested at an annual effective rate of interest equal to j in year one and an annual effective rate of discount equal to k in year two. Calculate the value of investment Z at the end of two years.
solution_steps: Investment X is invested at a nominal rate of interest \(j\), convertible semiannually, so the rate per half-year is \(j/2\).
There are \(4(2)=8\) semiannual periods in four years.
Dividing by \(100,000\), we get \(\left(1+\frac{j}{2}\right)^8=2.1435888\).
Taking the eighth root gives \(1+\frac{j}{2}=2.1435888^{1/8}\).
Investment Y is invested at a nominal rate of discount \(k\), convertible quarterly, so the discount rate per quarter is \(k/4\).
There are \(2(4)=8\) quarterly periods in two years.
For a nominal discount rate, the accumulation factor per quarter is \(\left(1-\frac{k}{4}\right)^{-1}\).
Taking both sides to the power \(-1/8\), we get \(1-\frac{k}{4}=2.3230573^{-1/8}\).
Investment Z earns an annual effective interest rate equal to \(j=0.20\) in year one, so the first-year accumulation factor is \(1.20\).
In year two, investment Z earns an annual effective discount rate equal to \(k=0.40\), so the one-year accumulation factor is \(\frac{1}{1-k}=\frac{1}{0.60}\).
generalization: For a nominal interest rate convertible m-thly, the accumulation factor over t years is \(\left(1+\frac{i^{(m)}}{m}\right)^{mt}\). For a nominal discount rate convertible m-thly, the accumulation factor over t years is \(\left(1-\frac{d^{(m)}}{m}\right)^{-mt}\).

title: Exercise 270
statement: The derivative of the force of interest with respect to the annual effective interest rate is denoted by dδ/di. The derivative of the annual effective interest rate with respect to the annual effective discount rate is denoted by di/dd. Simplify the expression (dδ/di)(di/dd).
solution_steps: The force of interest corresponding to an annual effective interest rate \(i\) is \(\delta=\ln(1+i)\).
Therefore, \(\frac{d}{di}\delta=\frac{d}{di}\ln(1+i)=\frac{1}{1+i}\).
Since \(v=\frac{1}{1+i}\), we have \(\frac{d}{di}\delta=v\).
The relationship between the annual effective interest rate \(i\) and the annual effective discount rate \(d\) is \(i=\frac{d}{1-d}\).
Differentiate \(i\) with respect to \(d\): \(\frac{d}{dd}i=\frac{d}{dd}\left(\frac{d}{1-d}\right)\).
Using the quotient rule, \(\frac{d}{dd}i=\frac{(1-d)(1)-d(-1)}{(1-d)^2}\).
Thus, \(\frac{d}{dd}i=\frac{1}{(1-d)^2}\).
Since \(v=1-d\), we get \(\frac{d}{dd}i=\frac{1}{v^2}\).
Therefore, \(\left(\frac{d}{di}\delta\right)\left(\frac{d}{dd}i\right)=v\cdot \frac{1}{v^2}=\frac{1}{v}\).
Finally, since \(\frac{1}{v}=1+i\), the expression simplifies to \(1+i\).
generalization: Use the relationships \(\delta=\ln(1+i)\), \(v=\frac{1}{1+i}\), and \(i=\frac{d}{1-d}\). Differentiating these identities allows expressions involving derivatives of interest, discount, and force of interest to be simplified.
answer_text: D. 1+i

title: Exercise 271
statement: John deposits $1,000 into a fund. The fund earns: (i) an annual nominal rate of interest of 4% convertible quarterly for the first three years; (ii) a constant annual force of interest of 5% for the next three years; and (iii) an annual nominal discount rate of 6% convertible semiannually thereafter. Calculate the amount in the fund at the end of ten years.
solution_steps: For the first three years, the fund earns an annual nominal rate of interest of 4% convertible quarterly, so the quarterly rate is \(0.04/4=0.01\).
There are \(3(4)=12\) quarters in the first three years, so the first accumulation factor is \(\left(1+\frac{0.04}{4}\right)^{12}\).
For the next three years, the fund earns a constant annual force of interest of 5%. The accumulation factor under a constant force of interest is \(e^{\delta t}\).
Here, \(\delta=0.05\) and \(t=3\), so the second accumulation factor is \(e^{0.05(3)}\).
After six years, there are four years remaining until the end of year ten.
During the last four years, the fund earns an annual nominal discount rate of 6% convertible semiannually, so the semiannual discount rate is \(0.06/2=0.03\).
For a nominal discount rate, the accumulation factor per conversion period is \(\frac{1}{1-d}\).
There are \(4(2)=8\) semiannual periods in the last four years, so the last accumulation factor is \(\left(1-\frac{0.06}{2}\right)^{-8}\).
Therefore, the amount at the end of ten years is \(1000\left(1+\frac{0.04}{4}\right)^{12}e^{0.05(3)}\left(1-\frac{0.06}{2}\right)^{-8}\).
The closest answer is \(1,670\).
generalization: When different interest accumulation methods apply over different time intervals, multiply the corresponding accumulation factors. For nominal interest, use \(\left(1+\frac{i^{(m)}}{m}\right)^{mt}\). For force of interest, use \(e^{\delta t}\). For nominal discount, use \(\left(1-\frac{d^{(m)}}{m}\right)^{-mt}\).

title: Exercise 272
statement: Which of the following are correct expressions for d^(360)? (I) 1 - v^(1/360). (II) 360 - 360(1 + i^(12)/12)^(-30). (III) 1 / (1/360 + 1/i^(360)). Let v = 1/(1+i).
choices: The correct answer is not given by A, B, C, or D
solution_steps: For a nominal discount rate \(d^{(m)}\) convertible \(m\)-thly, \(v=\left(1-\frac{d^{(m)}}{m}\right)^m\).
Therefore, \(v^{1/m}=1-\frac{d^{(m)}}{m}\), so \(d^{(m)}=m\left(1-v^{1/m}\right)\).
Statement (I) is false because \(1-v^{1/360}=\frac{d^{(360)}}{360}\), not \(d^{(360)}\).
For a nominal interest rate \(i^{(12)}\) convertible monthly, \(1+i=\left(1+\frac{i^{(12)}}{12}\right)^{12}\).
Statement (II) is false because it uses the exponent \(-30\) instead of \(-1/30\).
The relationship between nominal interest and nominal discount rates convertible \(m\)-thly is \(\frac{1}{d^{(m)}}-\frac{1}{i^{(m)}}=\frac{1}{m}\).
Taking reciprocals gives \(d^{(360)}=\frac{1}{\frac{1}{360}+\frac{1}{i^{(360)}}}\), so statement (III) is true.
Only statement (III) is correct.
generalization: For nominal discount convertible m-thly, use \(d^{(m)}=m(1-v^{1/m})\). Also, nominal interest and nominal discount convertible at the same frequency satisfy \(\frac{1}{d^{(m)}}-\frac{1}{i^{(m)}}=\frac{1}{m}\).
answer_text: D. III only

title: Exercise 273
statement: A store purchased couch #1 for X two months ago and plans to sell it for $1,500 six months from today. The same store purchases couch #2 for X today and plans to sell it for $1,500 four months from today. The annual force of interest is a constant 10%. The current value of the store's cash flows from the purchase and sale of couch #2 is 260. Calculate the current value of the store's cash flows from the purchase and sale of couch #1.
solution_steps: The annual force of interest is constant at \(\delta=0.10\).
Under a constant force of interest, a cash flow \(C\) at time \(t\) has current value \(Ce^{-\delta t}\), where \(t\) is measured in years.
For couch #2, the purchase occurs today, so the current value of the purchase is \(-X\).
The sale of couch #2 occurs four months from today, which is \(4/12\) years from today. Its current value is \(1500e^{-0.10(4/12)}\).
The current value of couch #2 is given as 260, so \(1500e^{-0.10(4/12)}-X=260\).
For couch #1, the purchase occurred two months ago, which is \(-2/12\) years relative to today. Its value at the current time is the accumulated value \(-Xe^{0.10(2/12)}\).
The sale of couch #1 occurs six months from today, which is \(6/12\) years from today. Its current value is \(1500e^{-0.10(6/12)}\).
Therefore, the current value of the cash flows from couch #1 is \(1500e^{-0.10(6/12)}-Xe^{0.10(2/12)}\).
generalization: With a constant force of interest \(\delta\), move cash flows to the valuation date using \(e^{-\delta t}\) for future cash flows and \(e^{\delta t}\) for past cash flows accumulated to the present.
